docs.title: HasProperty
docs.text:
Checks whether a value exposes a named property.

HasProperty() returns .T. when the supplied value exposes the named property and .F. when it does not. It is an existence check only; it does not read or assign the property's value.

The function throws if oTarget or sPropName is NIL. It is most useful with dynamic objects and other values that support property access.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When verifying that an object meets a specific schema or has required properties before performing operations that depend on them.
  • When validating input or output objects to ensure essential fields are present before processing.
  • When writing robust code that distinguishes missing properties from properties set to NIL or default values.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: HasProperty(oTarget, sPropName)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description oTarget object yes — The value to inspect for a property sPropName string yes — The property name to look up

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: boolean — .T. if the property exists on the supplied value, .F. otherwise

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text: Trigger Exception message oTarget is NIL. Argument oTarget cannot be null. sPropName is NIL. Argument sPropName cannot be null.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use HasProperty() before reading optional properties from dynamic objects.
  • Keep property names consistent across AddProperty, HasProperty(), and SetByName calls.
  • Treat HasProperty() as a presence check only, then read the value separately.

Don't

  • Pass NIL for oTarget or sPropName; the function raises an error instead of returning .F..
  • Use HasProperty to fetch a value. It only tells you whether the property exists.
  • Assume every SSL value supports property lookup. Values without properties can raise a runtime error.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text: • If the supplied value does not support property lookup, the call can raise a runtime error instead of returning .F..

docs.title: Check whether an optional property exists before reading it
docs.text:
Guards against a missing property before attempting to read it, then adds the property and shows that the same check now passes. Both branches produce distinct messages, demonstrating that the check accurately reflects the object's current state.

:PROCEDURE SendUserNotification;
    :DECLARE oUserProfile, sNotificationText;

    oUserProfile := CreateUdObject();
    oUserProfile:Name := "Jane Analyst";
    oUserProfile:Department := "Quality Control";

    :IF !HasProperty(oUserProfile, "Email");
        sNotificationText := "Notification cannot be sent because Email is missing.";
        UsrMes(sNotificationText);
        /* Displays missing-email message;
    :ENDIF;

    AddProperty(oUserProfile, "Email");
    oUserProfile:Email := "jane.analyst@lab.example.com";

    :IF HasProperty(oUserProfile, "Email");
        sNotificationText := "Sending notification to " + oUserProfile:Email;
        UsrMes(sNotificationText);
        /* Displays email notification message;
    :ENDIF;

    :RETURN .T.;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("SendUserNotification");

docs.title: Update fields conditionally based on property presence
docs.text:
Iterates over several properties, updating only those that exist on the object. LastModified was never added, so its branch is skipped and only two fields are logged as updated.

:PROCEDURE UpdateSampleFields;
    :DECLARE oSample, sStatus, aUpdatedFields, sLog;

    sStatus := "ACTIVE";
    aUpdatedFields := {};

    oSample := CreateUdObject();
    AddProperty(oSample, {"SampleID", "Status", "Notes"});
    oSample:SampleID := "LAB-2024-0042";
    oSample:Notes := "Initial review complete";

    :IF HasProperty(oSample, "Status");
        oSample:Status := sStatus;
        AAdd(aUpdatedFields, "Status");
    :ENDIF;

    :IF HasProperty(oSample, "Notes");
        oSample:Notes := "Updated via batch process";
        AAdd(aUpdatedFields, "Notes");
    :ENDIF;

    :IF HasProperty(oSample, "LastModified");
        oSample:LastModified := Today();
        AAdd(aUpdatedFields, "LastModified");
    :ENDIF;

    sLog := "Fields updated: " + LimsString(ALen(aUpdatedFields));
    UsrMes(sLog);

    :RETURN aUpdatedFields;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("UpdateSampleFields");

UsrMes displays:

Fields updated: 2

docs.title: HashData
docs.text:
Computes a one-way hash string from input text.

HashData hashes sInputData and returns the result as a hexadecimal string. If sAlgorithm is omitted or passed as NIL, the function uses "SH1". The only supported algorithm names are "SH1" and "MD5". Passing NIL for sInputData raises an error. Passing an unsupported algorithm raises an error.

Use HashData when you need a repeatable, non-reversible fingerprint of a string. For reversible protection, use EncryptData and DecryptData instead.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When you need to compare a current string value to a previously stored hash.
  • When an existing STARLIMS workflow expects SH1 or MD5 hashes.
  • When you need a one-way transformation rather than reversible encryption.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: HashData(sInputData, [sAlgorithm])

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description sInputData string yes — String value to hash. Passing NIL raises an error. An empty string is allowed and hashes successfully. sAlgorithm string no SH1 Hash algorithm name. Supported values are SH1 and MD5. If omitted or NIL, SH1 is used.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: string — An uppercase hexadecimal hash string for sInputData.

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text: Trigger Exception message sInputData is NIL. HashData. (Note: the runtime uses the function name as the exception message text — it is not descriptive.) sAlgorithm is not "SH1" or "MD5". Invalid Hashing Algorithm

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Standardize on one algorithm for a given workflow so stored hashes remain comparable.
  • Handle runtime errors when sInputData may be NIL or sAlgorithm may come from external input.
  • Record which algorithm produced the hash when values may be shared across systems.

Don't

  • Pass algorithm names such as SHA1 or SHA256. HashData only accepts SH1 and MD5.
  • Switch algorithms for existing stored hashes without a migration plan. The same input produces different hashes under different algorithms.
  • Use HashData when you need to recover the original value later. Use EncryptData for reversible storage.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• sAlgorithm is case-insensitive, but the accepted values are still limited to SH1 and MD5.
  • HashData("") is valid and returns the hash of the empty string.
  • The same input hashed with SH1 and MD5 produces different results.

docs.title: Hash a value with the default algorithm
docs.text:
Hash a string with the default SH1 algorithm and compare it to a known hash.

:PROCEDURE VerifySampleToken;
    :DECLARE sToken, sExpectedHash, sActualHash;

    sToken := "LAB-2026-0001";
    sExpectedHash := HashData("LAB-2026-0001");
    sActualHash := HashData(sToken);

    :IF sActualHash == sExpectedHash;
        UsrMes("Token hash matched");
        :RETURN .T.;
    :ENDIF;

    UsrMes("Token hash did not match");

    :RETURN .F.;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("VerifySampleToken");

docs.title: Use an explicit algorithm for an external interface
docs.text:
Specify MD5 explicitly when an existing integration expects that algorithm.

:PROCEDURE BuildLegacyHashRecord;
    :DECLARE sPayload, sHash, oRecord;

    sPayload := "sample=LAB-2026-0001|status=Logged|user=apiuser";
    sHash := HashData(sPayload, "MD5");

    oRecord := CreateUdObject();
    oRecord:payload := sPayload;
    oRecord:algorithm := "MD5";
    oRecord:hash := sHash;

    :RETURN oRecord;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("BuildLegacyHashRecord");

docs.title: Validate user-supplied algorithm input
docs.text:
Catch the runtime error when the requested algorithm is not supported.

:PROCEDURE SafeHashWithRequestedAlgorithm;
    :PARAMETERS sInputData, sAlgorithm;
    :DECLARE sHash, oErr;

    :TRY;
        sHash := HashData(sInputData, sAlgorithm);

        :RETURN sHash;
    :CATCH;
        oErr := GetLastSSLError();
        UsrMes("Hashing failed: " + oErr:Description);
        /* Displays on failure: hashing failed with the runtime error;

        :RETURN "";
    :ENDTRY;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("SafeHashWithRequestedAlgorithm", {"my-data", "SHA256"});

docs.title: Related

• DecryptData
  • EncryptData
  • string

docs.title: Hour
docs.text:
Extracts the hour component from a date value.

Hour returns the hour portion of a date as a number from 0 to 23. If the input date is empty, the function returns 0. If the argument is NIL, or if the supplied value is not a date, the function raises an error.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When you need time-of-day logic based on a timestamp's hour value.
  • When you need to group or count records by hour.
  • When you need the hour without separately parsing a formatted date string.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: Hour(dDate)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description dDate date yes — Date value to inspect

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: number — The hour component of dDate, from 0 to 23. Returns 0 when dDate is empty.

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text: Trigger Exception message dDate is NIL. Argument: dDate cannot be null. dDate is not a date value. Argument: dDate must be of date type

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Pass a real date value before calling Hour.
  • Treat a returned 0 carefully when empty dates are possible, because midnight also returns 0.
  • Combine Hour with Minute or Second when hour-only precision is not enough.

Don't

  • Pass NIL and expect 0; NIL raises an error.
  • Pass strings or other non-date values and expect implicit conversion.
  • Assume 0 always means midnight when your input may be empty.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text: • Midnight also returns 0, so the result alone does not distinguish midnight from an empty date.

docs.title: Choose a greeting based on the current hour
docs.text:
Call Hour on the current timestamp and use its value to select a time-of-day greeting. Because the procedure reads the live clock, the greeting and hour shown in the output are representative, and the actual values depend on when the procedure runs.

:PROCEDURE ShowLoginGreeting;
    :DECLARE dNow, nHour, sGreeting;

    dNow := Now();
    nHour := Hour(dNow);

    :BEGINCASE;
    :CASE nHour < 12;
        sGreeting := "Good morning";
        :EXITCASE;
    :CASE nHour < 17;
        sGreeting := "Good afternoon";
        :EXITCASE;
    :OTHERWISE;
        sGreeting := "Good evening";
        :EXITCASE;
    :ENDCASE;

    UsrMes(sGreeting + ". Current hour: " + LimsString(nHour));
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ShowLoginGreeting");

UsrMes displays:

Good morning. Current hour: 9

docs.title: Count events into 24 hourly buckets
docs.text:
Build a 24-element frequency array by extracting the hour from each event's timestamp and incrementing the corresponding slot. This shows how Hour converts a full timestamp into an array index for time-based aggregation.

:PROCEDURE CountEventsByHour;
    :DECLARE aEvents, aHourCounts, nIndex, nHour, nSlot;

    aEvents := {
        {DateFromNumbers(2026, 4, 18, 8, 15, 0), "Login"},
        {DateFromNumbers(2026, 4, 18, 8, 42, 0), "Login"},
        {DateFromNumbers(2026, 4, 18, 9, 5, 0), "Approve"},
        {DateFromNumbers(2026, 4, 18, 17, 30, 0), "Release"}
    };

    aHourCounts := ArrayNew(24);

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO 24;
        aHourCounts[nIndex] := 0;
    :NEXT;

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aEvents);
        nHour := Hour(aEvents[nIndex, 1]);
        nSlot := nHour + 1;
        aHourCounts[nSlot] := aHourCounts[nSlot] + 1;
    :NEXT;

    :RETURN aHourCounts;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("CountEventsByHour");

docs.title: Route requests based on whether they fall inside business hours
docs.text:
Classify a list of timestamped requests as either ready to process or queued, using Hour to test each timestamp against a configurable business-hours window. Requests whose hour falls before nOpenHour or at or after nCloseHour are assigned the QUEUE status.

:PROCEDURE ClassifyRequestsByHour;
    :DECLARE aRequests, aStatuses, nIndex, dRequestedAt, nHour;
    :DECLARE nOpenHour, nCloseHour;

    aRequests := {
        {"REQ-1001", DateFromNumbers(2026, 4, 18, 7, 45, 0)},
        {"REQ-1002", DateFromNumbers(2026, 4, 18, 10, 0, 0)},
        {"REQ-1003", DateFromNumbers(2026, 4, 18, 18, 10, 0)}
    };

    aStatuses := {};
    nOpenHour := 9;
    nCloseHour := 17;

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aRequests);
        dRequestedAt := aRequests[nIndex, 2];
        nHour := Hour(dRequestedAt);

        :IF nHour >= nOpenHour .AND. nHour < nCloseHour;
            AAdd(aStatuses, {aRequests[nIndex, 1], "PROCESS_NOW", nHour});
        :ELSE;
            AAdd(aStatuses, {aRequests[nIndex, 1], "QUEUE", nHour});
        :ENDIF;
    :NEXT;

    :RETURN aStatuses;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ClassifyRequestsByHour");

docs.title: HtmlDecode
docs.text:
Converts selected HTML/XML entity sequences in a string back to literal characters.

HtmlDecode accepts one string argument. When sData is NIL, the function returns NIL. Otherwise it replaces the following sequences and leaves all other text unchanged.

Entity sequence Decoded character < < > > ' ' " " & &

Use this function when you need to reverse output produced by HtmlEncode or to make encoded text readable in a plain-text context.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When a value was previously passed through HtmlEncode and now needs to be shown as normal text.
  • When external data contains encoded markup characters such as < or & and your script needs the literal characters.
  • When you want readable text for logging, comparison, or plain-text display instead of entity sequences.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: HtmlDecode(sData)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description sData string yes — String to decode. NIL is accepted and returns NIL.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: string - The decoded string with supported entity sequences replaced by their literal characters. Returns NIL when sData is NIL.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use HtmlDecode when you need to reverse text that was previously encoded with HtmlEncode.
  • Check for NIL when your calling code distinguishes between no value and an empty string.
  • Treat the decoded result as plain text unless you have separately validated it for HTML rendering.

Don't

  • Assume HtmlDecode sanitizes content. It only converts supported entity sequences back to literal characters.
  • Assume it decodes every named HTML entity. The implementation only replaces <, >, ', ", and &.
  • Inject decoded untrusted content into an HTML-rendering context without separate validation or sanitization.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text: • An empty string returns an empty string.

docs.title: Decode text for plain display
docs.text:
Convert encoded comparison text into a readable message.

:PROCEDURE ShowDecodedMessage;
    :DECLARE sEncodedText, sDecodedText;

    sEncodedText := "Formula requires x < 100 & y > 0";
    sDecodedText := HtmlDecode(sEncodedText);

    UsrMes(sDecodedText);

    :RETURN sDecodedText;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ShowDecodedMessage");

UsrMes displays:

Formula requires x < 100 & y > 0

docs.title: Decode a list of encoded values
docs.text:
Process multiple encoded strings, showing that only the five supported entity sequences are decoded. The third entry uses ±, which is not a supported entity, so it is returned unchanged and UsrMes displays Value ± limit.

:PROCEDURE DecodeMessages;
    :DECLARE aEncoded, aDecoded, sMessage;
    :DECLARE nIndex;

    aEncoded := {
        "Sample <A>",
        "Tom & Jerry",
        "Value ± limit"
    };
    aDecoded := {};

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aEncoded);
        sMessage := HtmlDecode(aEncoded[nIndex]);
        AAdd(aDecoded, sMessage);
    :NEXT;

    UsrMes(aDecoded[1]);
    UsrMes(aDecoded[2]);
    UsrMes(aDecoded[3]);

    :RETURN aDecoded;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("DecodeMessages");

UsrMes displays:

Sample <A>
Tom & Jerry
Value ± limit

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• HtmlEncode
  • string

docs.title: HtmlEncode
docs.text:
Converts selected characters in a string to entity sequences for HTML or XML text output.

HtmlEncode accepts one string argument. When sData is NIL, the function returns NIL. Otherwise it replaces the following characters and leaves all other text unchanged.

Character Entity sequence & & < < > > ' ' " "

Use this function when text must be inserted into HTML or XML content as literal text instead of markup. Use HtmlDecode to reverse these specific replacements.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When inserting text into generated HTML or XML content and the text may contain <, >, &, quotes, or apostrophes.
  • When building markup strings from data that should be treated as text rather than interpreted as tags or attributes.
  • When you need the exact entity replacements supported by HtmlEncode before passing the result to another system.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: HtmlEncode(sData)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description sData string yes — String to encode. NIL is accepted and returns NIL.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: string — The encoded string with &, <, >, ', and " replaced by their entity sequences. Returns NIL when sData is NIL.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Encode text at the point where you insert it into HTML or XML output.
  • Keep the original unencoded value when that same data may also be needed for plain text, storage, or later editing.
  • Use HtmlDecode only when you specifically need to undo the five replacements performed by HtmlEncode.

Don't

  • Encode the same value multiple times unless you intentionally want sequences like &amp;.
  • Assume HtmlEncode performs general input validation or business rule checks. It only replaces five characters.
  • Assume it converts every named HTML entity or every non-ASCII character. Characters outside the five supported replacements are left as-is.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• An empty string returns an empty string.
  • Already encoded text is encoded again. For example, & becomes &amp;.

docs.title: Encode a string before inserting it into HTML
docs.text:
Encode user-supplied text before placing it inside a markup string, so that special characters become safe entity sequences rather than markup.

:PROCEDURE BuildCommentMarkup;
    :DECLARE sComment, sEncodedComment, sHtml;

    sComment := "Meeting at 3pm <review> & 'coffee'";
    sEncodedComment := HtmlEncode(sComment);
    sHtml := "<p>" + sEncodedComment + "</p>";

    UsrMes(sHtml);

    :RETURN sHtml;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("BuildCommentMarkup");

UsrMes displays:

<p>Meeting at 3pm <review> & 'coffee'</p>

docs.title: Encode attribute values when building an XML element
docs.text:
Encode each attribute value inline while composing an XML element string, so that quotes and special characters inside values do not break the markup structure.

:PROCEDURE BuildSampleXml;
    :DECLARE sSampleId, sStatus, sXml;

    sSampleId := "A&B<42>";
    sStatus := 'Ready "now"';
    sXml := "<sample id='" + HtmlEncode(sSampleId) + "' status='"
            + HtmlEncode(sStatus) + "'></sample>";

    UsrMes(sXml);

    :RETURN sXml;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("BuildSampleXml");

UsrMes displays:

<sample id='A&B<42>' status='Ready "now"'></sample>

docs.title: Encode a batch of values including NIL entries
docs.text:
Encode an array containing a mix of strings and a NIL entry, showing that HtmlEncode passes NIL through unchanged while encoding all other values. After the loop, aEncodedNotes[1] holds <alpha>, entry 2 remains NIL, aEncodedNotes[3] holds Tom & Jerry, and aEncodedNotes[4] holds She said "go". The UsrMes call confirms that the NIL entry was preserved.

:PROCEDURE EncodeNotes;
    :DECLARE aNotes, aEncodedNotes;
    :DECLARE nIndex, vEncoded;

    aNotes := {"<alpha>", NIL, "Tom & Jerry", 'She said "go"'};
    aEncodedNotes := {};

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aNotes);
        vEncoded := HtmlEncode(aNotes[nIndex]);
        AAdd(aEncodedNotes, vEncoded);
    :NEXT;

    UsrMes(LimsString(Empty(aEncodedNotes[2])));

    :RETURN aEncodedNotes;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("EncodeNotes");

UsrMes displays:

true

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• HtmlDecode
  • string

docs.title: IIf
docs.text:
Selects one of two values based on a boolean condition.

IIf returns vTrueValue when bCondition is true and vFalseValue when bCondition is false. The condition argument must be a boolean value and cannot be NIL. The second and third arguments can be any value type.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When you need a compact two-way value choice inside an assignment or return.
  • When both possible values are already available as literals or variables.
  • When using a full :IF block would make a simple expression harder to read.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: IIf(bCondition, vTrueValue, vFalseValue)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description bCondition boolean yes — Condition indicating which value to return vTrueValue any yes — Value to return if bCondition is true vFalseValue any yes — Value to return if bCondition is false

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: any — vTrueValue when bCondition is .T.; otherwise vFalseValue.

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text: Trigger Exception message bCondition is NIL. Null argument passed to IIf() The call does not supply exactly three arguments. Invalid number of arguments for function IIF.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use IIf for short, direct two-way value selection.
  • Pass a boolean expression for bCondition.
  • Prefer literals or previously assigned variables for vTrueValue and vFalseValue.

Don't

  • Pass NIL as bCondition.
  • Rely on IIf to avoid evaluating the unused branch.
  • Build long nested IIf chains when a regular :IF block is clearer.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• IIf is a normal function call. Its arguments are evaluated before the function returns either vTrueValue or vFalseValue.
  • The compiler validates IIf calls specially and raises a compile-time error when the call does not contain exactly three arguments.
  • The compiler can warn when the second or third argument is not a constant or a variable name.

docs.title: Assign a label based on a boolean flag
docs.text:
Select a status label for display based on a boolean flag, showing the most direct use of IIf as a compact conditional assignment.

:PROCEDURE GetApprovalStatus;
    :DECLARE bIsApproved, sStatusLabel;

    bIsApproved := .T.;
    sStatusLabel := IIf(bIsApproved, "Approved", "Pending Review");

    UsrMes(sStatusLabel);

    :RETURN sStatusLabel;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("GetApprovalStatus");

UsrMes displays:

Approved

docs.title: Select a prepared SQL filter
docs.text:
Build a SQL filter clause dynamically by selecting between two pre-prepared filter strings, showing how IIf can be embedded directly inside a string concatenation expression.

:PROCEDURE BuildSampleQuery;
    :DECLARE bLoggedOnly, sStatusFilter, sDateFilter, sSQL;

    bLoggedOnly := .T.;
    sStatusFilter := "status = 'Logged'";
    sDateFilter := "logdate >= SYSDATE - 7";

    sSQL := "
        SELECT sampleid, status, logdate
        FROM sample
        WHERE " + IIf(bLoggedOnly, sStatusFilter, sDateFilter) + "
        ORDER BY logdate DESC
    ";

    :RETURN sSQL;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("BuildSampleQuery");

docs.title: Prepare both branch values before calling IIf
docs.text:
Illustrates the recommended style: compute both branch values before the IIf call rather than inline, making both branches easier to read and avoiding complex expressions inside the call itself.

:PROCEDURE GetReportLabel;
    :DECLARE bUseDetailed, sShortLabel, sDetailedLabel, sLabel;

    bUseDetailed := .T.;

    sShortLabel := "QC Summary";
    sDetailedLabel := "QC Summary - Includes exception details";

    sLabel := IIf(bUseDetailed, sDetailedLabel, sShortLabel);

    :RETURN sLabel;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("GetReportLabel");

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• IF
  • BEGINCASE
  • boolean

docs.title: IgnoreSqlErrors
docs.text:
Enables or disables SQL error suppression and returns the previous setting.

IgnoreSqlErrors(bEnable) sets the current SQL error suppression flag to the boolean value you pass and returns the previous value. This makes it suitable for temporary changes around a specific block of database work, where you want best-effort SQL execution and then a clean restore of the earlier behavior.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When you are running best-effort cleanup where some SQL statements may fail and the rest of the work should continue.
  • When you need to suppress SQL errors only for a short, controlled section of code and then restore the prior setting.
  • When you are coordinating SQL error suppression with ShowSqlErrors for quieter maintenance or cleanup flows.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: IgnoreSqlErrors(bEnable)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description bEnable boolean yes — .T. enables SQL error suppression. .F. restores normal SQL error throwing.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: boolean — The previous SQL error suppression setting.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Save the returned value and restore it after the temporary SQL work is complete.
  • Limit suppression to a small, clearly defined block of database operations.
  • Add a brief comment explaining why the SQL work is intentionally best-effort.

Don't

  • Leave SQL error suppression enabled longer than necessary because later SQL failures can be hidden.
  • Use this as a default error-handling strategy because it can mask real database problems.
  • Change this flag without considering ShowSqlErrors when you need predictable SQL error visibility.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• The setting remains in effect for later SQL operations until you change it again.
  • If you forget to restore the previous value, unrelated SQL work later in the same execution flow may continue with suppression still enabled.
  • SQL error suppression is not a substitute for validation, transaction control, or explicit post-operation checks.
  • This setting works together with ShowSqlErrors; some SQL errors classified as fatal may still be raised when SQL error display is enabled.

docs.title: Suppress errors for a batch of best-effort deletes
docs.text:
Shows the save-and-restore pattern: capture the previous value before enabling suppression, run best-effort deletes where any individual failure is intentionally ignored, then restore the original setting so later SQL operations are unaffected.

:PROCEDURE CleanupOptionalAuditRows;
    :DECLARE bPrevIgnore, sSql, aSampleIds, nIndex;

    bPrevIgnore := IgnoreSqlErrors(.T.);

    sSql := "
        DELETE FROM sample_audit
        WHERE sample_id = ?
    ";
    aSampleIds := {"S-1001", "S-1002", "S-9999"};

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aSampleIds);
        RunSQL(sSql,, {aSampleIds[nIndex]});
    :NEXT;

    IgnoreSqlErrors(bPrevIgnore);
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("CleanupOptionalAuditRows");

docs.title: Restore the previous setting with :FINALLY
docs.text:
Uses :FINALLY to guarantee restoration even if the archive SQL throws. Capturing the return value before the :TRY block and restoring it in :FINALLY prevents the suppression flag from being stranded if an error escapes the block.

:PROCEDURE ArchiveCompletedTasks;
    :DECLARE bPrevIgnore, sInsertSql, sDeleteSql, dCutoff;

    dCutoff := Today() - 30;
    bPrevIgnore := IgnoreSqlErrors(.T.);

    :TRY;
        sInsertSql := "
            INSERT INTO ordtask_archive
                (ordno, testcode, status, logdate)
            SELECT ordno, testcode, status, logdate
            FROM ordtask
            WHERE status = ?
              AND logdate < ?
            ";
        RunSQL(sInsertSql,, {"Complete", dCutoff});

        sDeleteSql := "
            DELETE FROM ordtask
            WHERE status = ?
              AND logdate < ?
            ";
        RunSQL(sDeleteSql,, {"Complete", dCutoff});
    :FINALLY;
        IgnoreSqlErrors(bPrevIgnore);
    :ENDTRY;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ArchiveCompletedTasks");

docs.title: Coordinate SQL suppression and error display together
docs.text:
Combines IgnoreSqlErrors with ShowSqlErrors to make a maintenance window fully quiet. Both settings are saved before the block and restored in :FINALLY in reverse order, display restored first and then suppression, to match the reverse of how they were set.

:PROCEDURE RunQuietMaintenance;
    :DECLARE bPrevIgnore, bPrevShow, sDeleteSql, sUpdateSql, dCutoff;

    dCutoff := Today() - 7;
    bPrevIgnore := IgnoreSqlErrors(.T.);
    bPrevShow := ShowSqlErrors(.F.);

    :TRY;
        sDeleteSql := "
            DELETE FROM temp_results
            WHERE logdate < ?
            ";
        RunSQL(sDeleteSql,, {dCutoff});

        sUpdateSql := "
            UPDATE batch_queue SET
                status = ?
            WHERE status = ?
              AND startdate < ?
            ";
        RunSQL(sUpdateSql,, {"Expired", "Pending", dCutoff});
    :FINALLY;
        ShowSqlErrors(bPrevShow);
        IgnoreSqlErrors(bPrevIgnore);
    :ENDTRY;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("RunQuietMaintenance");

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• SetSqlTimeout
  • ShowSqlErrors
  • boolean

docs.title: InBatchProcess
docs.text:
Returns whether the current SSL execution context is running in a batch process.

InBatchProcess() is a parameterless function that reads the current runtime context and returns a boolean result. Use it when the same script needs one path for background batch execution and another for interactive execution.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When a script should suppress user-facing prompts during background work.
  • When you need different logging, notification, or follow-up behavior in batch and interactive runs.
  • When writing shared code that may run both from a user action and from a submitted batch job.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: InBatchProcess()

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: This function takes no parameters.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: boolean — .T. when the current execution context is running in batch process mode; otherwise .F..

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use InBatchProcess() to choose between batch-safe and interactive behavior.
  • Use quieter logging in batch code when a popup or prompt would be inappropriate.
  • Keep the branch close to the behavior it controls so the context decision stays easy to follow.

Don't

  • Track batch state with your own flag when the runtime can answer it directly.
  • Assume code always runs interactively if the same routine can also be submitted to batch.
  • Use ErrorMes for ordinary context reporting when UsrMes or InfoMes is sufficient.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• InBatchProcess() only tells you whether the current context is in batch mode.
  • The function does not identify a batch job or provide batch metadata.

docs.title: Skip a popup during batch execution
docs.text:
Use a non-interactive message in batch and a user popup in interactive execution, showing the most direct application of the InBatchProcess() check.

:PROCEDURE NotifyCompletion;
    :DECLARE bIsBatch, sMessage;

    bIsBatch := InBatchProcess();
    sMessage := "Sample release processing completed";

    :IF bIsBatch;
        /* Batch run uses non-interactive logging;
        InfoMes(sMessage);
    :ELSE;
        /* Interactive run can show a user message;
        UsrMes(sMessage);
    :ENDIF;

    :RETURN bIsBatch;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("NotifyCompletion");

docs.title: Reuse one procedure in both interactive and batch paths
docs.text:
Execute the work immediately when already in batch; submit it to a new batch job otherwise. The batch ID returned by SubmitToBatchEx is system-assigned and varies per call.

:PROCEDURE ProcessQueuedAudit;
    :DECLARE bIsBatch, sCode, sBatchId;

    bIsBatch := InBatchProcess();

    :IF bIsBatch;
        /* Already in batch so do the work now;
        InfoMes("Running audit work inside batch context");
    :ELSE;
        sCode := "InfoMes('Running audit work inside batch context');";
        sBatchId := SubmitToBatchEx(sCode);

        /* Displays submitted batch ID;
        UsrMes("Submitted audit work to batch: " + sBatchId);
    :ENDIF;

    :RETURN .T.;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ProcessQueuedAudit");

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• BatchSupport
  • SubmitToBatch
  • SubmitToBatchEx
  • boolean

docs.title: InfoMes
docs.text:
Logs an informational user message and returns the same formatted string as UsrMes.

InfoMes uses the same message behavior as UsrMes. It accepts the same two arguments, converts them to strings, applies the same fallback handling for empty values, and returns the formatted message text. Unlike ErrorMes, it does not force the message to be written when regular user-message logging is disabled.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When you want code to clearly express that a message is informational rather than an error.
  • When you want the same caption-and-detail behavior as UsrMes.
  • When you want the formatted message string returned to your code.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: InfoMes(vCaption, [vMessage])

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description vCaption any yes — Caption text, or the message content when vMessage is omitted or empty. vMessage any no — Message body or additional detail text.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: string — The formatted message string. If ordinary user-message logging is disabled, InfoMes returns an empty string.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Pass a short caption in vCaption and the detail text in vMessage.
  • Use InfoMes for routine progress notes, confirmations, and other non-error messages.
  • Use ErrorMes when the message must still be written during error handling.

Don't

  • Use InfoMes for failures that must always be recorded.
  • Pass arrays or objects unless their string form is exactly what you want returned and logged.
  • Rely on InfoMes as a guaranteed audit mechanism when ordinary user-message logging may be disabled.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Both arguments are converted to strings before the message is processed.
  • If vCaption is empty, a default user-message caption is used.
  • If vMessage is empty, the value from vCaption becomes the message body and the caption is reset to the default user-message caption.
  • InfoMes follows the same write behavior as UsrMes, so it can return an empty string when ordinary user-message logging is disabled.

docs.title: Log a simple informational message
docs.text:
Use a stable caption and a short detail message for routine status reporting.

:PROCEDURE ConfirmRegistration;
    :DECLARE sSampleID, sMessage;

    sSampleID := "SMP-2024-0042";
    sMessage := "Sample " + sSampleID + " was registered successfully";

    InfoMes("Registration Complete", sMessage);
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ConfirmRegistration");

docs.title: Capture the returned message text
docs.text:
Store the returned string when later code needs the fully formatted message.

:PROCEDURE RecordImportSummary;
    :PARAMETERS sBatchID, nImported;
    :DECLARE sCaption, sMessage, sLogged;

    sCaption := "Import Summary";
    sMessage := "Batch " + sBatchID + " imported ";
    sMessage := sMessage + LimsString(nImported) + " rows";

    sLogged := InfoMes(sCaption, sMessage);

    :RETURN sLogged;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("RecordImportSummary", {"BATCH-001", 42});

docs.title: Report per-item progress in a loop
docs.text:
Use InfoMes for per-item progress updates while preserving a final summary for the caller.

:PROCEDURE RefreshSampleCache;
    :PARAMETERS aSampleIDs;
    :DECLARE nIndex, sCaption, sMessage, sLastLogged;

    sCaption := "Cache Refresh";
    sLastLogged := "";

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aSampleIDs);
        sMessage := "Refreshing sample " + aSampleIDs[nIndex];
        sLastLogged := InfoMes(sCaption, sMessage);
    :NEXT;

    sMessage := "Refreshed " + LimsString(ALen(aSampleIDs)) + " samples";
    sLastLogged := InfoMes(sCaption, sMessage);

    :RETURN sLastLogged;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("RefreshSampleCache", {{"SMP-001", "SMP-002", "SMP-003"}});

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• UsrMes
  • ErrorMes
  • string

docs.title: Integer
docs.text:
Returns the whole-number portion of a numeric value by truncating the fractional part toward zero.

Integer takes one numeric argument and returns a numeric result. It does not round to the nearest value. Positive numbers are truncated downward toward zero, and negative numbers are truncated upward toward zero.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When you need truncation rather than rounding.
  • When you need to remove the fractional part before storing or displaying a whole-number value.
  • When you want predictable toward-zero behavior for negative numbers.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: Integer(nValue)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description nValue number yes — Numeric value to truncate

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: number — The input value with any fractional part removed.

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text: Trigger Exception message nValue is NIL. Value cannot be null. (Parameter 'nValue')

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use Integer when you specifically want truncation toward zero.
  • Validate string input before converting it to a number and passing it to Integer.
  • Use Round instead when you need rounding rather than truncation.

Don't

  • Treat Integer as a rounding function. It removes the fractional part instead.
  • Pass NIL and expect a blank or default numeric result.
  • Assume Integer(-2.7) behaves like floor logic. It returns -2, not -3.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text: • If you are starting from text input, convert it to a number first.

docs.title: Remove the fractional part from a price
docs.text:
Call Integer on a positive decimal to get the whole-number portion without any rounding.

:DECLARE nPrice, nWholePrice, sMessage;

nPrice := 99.95;
nWholePrice := Integer(nPrice);

sMessage := "Original: " + LimsString(nPrice)
            + ", Integer: " + LimsString(nWholePrice);

UsrMes(sMessage);

UsrMes displays:

Original: 99.95, Integer: 99

docs.title: Truncate a negative value toward zero
docs.text:
Integer(-2.7) returns -2, not -3, because truncation moves toward zero rather than toward negative infinity.

:DECLARE nRawValue, nTruncValue, sMessage;

nRawValue := -2.7;
nTruncValue := Integer(nRawValue);

sMessage := "Original: " + LimsString(nRawValue)
            + ", Integer: " + LimsString(nTruncValue);

UsrMes(sMessage);

UsrMes displays:

Original: -2.7, Integer: -2

docs.title: Validate text input before truncating
docs.text:
Validate user-supplied text with IsNumeric before converting it with Val and truncating, so that a bad input produces a clear message rather than a runtime error.

:PROCEDURE SubmitWholeNumber;
    :PARAMETERS sRawValue;
    :DECLARE nConvertedValue;

    :IF Empty(sRawValue) .OR. !IsNumeric(sRawValue);
        UsrMes("Enter a numeric value before calling Integer.");
        :RETURN;
    :ENDIF;

    nConvertedValue := Integer(Val(sRawValue));
    UsrMes("Submitting whole-number value: " + LimsString(nConvertedValue));
:ENDPROC;

DoProc("SubmitWholeNumber", {"3.7"});

UsrMes displays:

Submitting whole-number value: 3

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Round
  • Val
  • IsNumeric
  • number

docs.title: IsDBConnected
docs.text:
Checks whether STARLIMS currently has a database connection available for a given connection name.

If you pass a connection name, the function checks that named connection. If you omit the argument or pass an empty string, it checks the current default connection instead. The function returns .T. when a matching connection is available and .F. when it is not. It does not run a test query or validate the target database beyond that connection lookup.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When you need to check whether the default database connection is available before running database work.
  • When a script can target more than one configured connection and needs to verify a specific one by name.
  • When you want a quick connection-availability check without executing SQL.
  • When you need to branch between a fallback path and normal database processing.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: IsDBConnected([sConnectionName])

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description sConnectionName string no NIL Connection name of the database connection to check. If omitted or passed as an empty string, the function checks the current default connection.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: boolean — .T. if the named connection exists, or if the default connection exists when no name is supplied; otherwise .F.

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text: Trigger Exception message The runtime database collection has not been initialized. The internal database collection is null

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use IsDBConnected() before database work when your script needs a fast availability check.
  • Pass an explicit connection name when your script works with more than one connection.
  • Wrap the call in :TRY / :CATCH when the runtime context may not have database state available.

Don't

  • Treat this as a connectivity probe that validates credentials, network reachability, or a successful round-trip query.
  • Pass an empty string when you mean to verify a specific non-default connection by name.
  • Assume the function always returns .F. on failure; it can raise an error when runtime database state is unavailable.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• If the connection name does not match a known connection, the function returns .F..
  • If runtime database state is unavailable, the function raises an error instead of returning .F..
  • Passing an empty string checks the default connection, which may not be the named connection you intended to inspect.

docs.title: Guard a query with a default connection check
docs.text:
Check the default connection before executing a query, and exit early with a message when no connection is available. The success path shows that IsDBConnected() without an argument targets the default connection.

:PROCEDURE CheckDbBeforeQuery;
    :DECLARE bIsConnected, aResults;

    bIsConnected := IsDBConnected();

    :IF !bIsConnected;
        UsrMes("Default database connection is not available");
        :RETURN .F.;
    :ENDIF;

    aResults := SQLExecute("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sample");
    UsrMes("Sample count: " + LimsString(aResults[1, 1]));
    /* Displays the sample count when the query succeeds;

    :RETURN .T.;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("CheckDbBeforeQuery");

docs.title: Check availability of multiple named connections
docs.text:
Iterate over a list of connection names and report each one's availability. Passing an explicit name to IsDBConnected lets you verify named connections independently of the current default.

:PROCEDURE CheckMultiDatabaseConnections;
    :DECLARE aConnectionNames, sConnectionName, bIsConnected, sStatusMessage, nIndex;

    aConnectionNames := {"LIMS_MASTER", "LIMS_REPORTING", "LIMS_ARCHIVE"};

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aConnectionNames);
        sConnectionName := aConnectionNames[nIndex];
        bIsConnected := IsDBConnected(sConnectionName);

        :IF bIsConnected;
            sStatusMessage := sConnectionName + " is available";
        :ELSE;
            sStatusMessage := sConnectionName + " is not available";
        :ENDIF;

        UsrMes(sStatusMessage);
        /* Displays each connection's availability;
    :NEXT;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("CheckMultiDatabaseConnections");

docs.title: Handle uninitialized database state with :TRY
docs.text:
Wrap the call in :TRY / :CATCH for code paths where the runtime database collection may not be initialized. The catch block retrieves the error with GetLastSSLError and logs it via ErrorMes before returning a safe .F..

:PROCEDURE CheckDatabaseConnectivity;
    :PARAMETERS sConnName;
    :DEFAULT sConnName, "";
    :DECLARE bIsConnected, oErr;

    :TRY;
        bIsConnected := IsDBConnected(sConnName);

        :IF bIsConnected;
            UsrMes("Requested connection is available");
        :ELSE;
            UsrMes("Requested connection is not available");
        :ENDIF;

        :RETURN bIsConnected;
    :CATCH;
        oErr := GetLastSSLError();
        ErrorMes("Connection lookup failed: " + oErr:Description);
        /* Displays a failure message when the lookup raises an error;
        :RETURN .F.;
    :ENDTRY;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("CheckDatabaseConnectivity", {"LIMS_MASTER"});

docs.title: IsDefined
docs.text:
Determines whether a variable name is currently defined.

IsDefined accepts a variable name as a non-empty string and returns .T. when that name resolves in the current local scope, a caller scope, or public scope. It returns .F. when the name is not defined. If you pass NIL, an empty string, or a non-string value, the function raises an exception instead of returning .F..

Use IsDefined when the variable name is only known at runtime and you need to check for existence before reading it with functions such as GetByName or changing your script flow.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When a variable name comes from runtime data and you need to know whether it exists.
  • When you want to guard a later GetByName call.
  • When you support optional local or public variables in the same script flow.
  • When you need to branch differently depending on whether a setup variable was created.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: IsDefined(sVarName)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description sVarName string yes — Name of the variable to check for existence. Must be non-empty.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: boolean — .T. when the name resolves in the current local scope, a caller scope, or public scope; .F. when no variable with that name exists.

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text: Trigger Exception message sVarName is NIL. Argument 'sVarName' cannot be null! sVarName is not a string or is empty. Argument 'sVarName' must be a non-empty string!

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Pass a runtime variable name string, not the variable value itself.
  • Use IsDefined() before GetByName when a variable may or may not exist.
  • Use Empty separately when you need to know whether a defined variable also has content.

Don't

  • Pass NIL, "", or a non-string value and expect .F.. Those inputs raise exceptions.
  • Use IsDefined() to check object properties such as oConfig:ModuleName; it checks variable names, not member paths.
  • Use Empty as a substitute for existence checks. A variable can be defined and still be empty.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Variable lookup follows normal SSL variable resolution: current local scope, then caller scopes, then public scope.
  • The name comparison is case-insensitive, consistent with SSL variable names.
  • IsDefined() checks whether the variable exists, not whether its value is empty.

docs.title: Confirm a variable is defined before reading it
docs.text:
Check with IsDefined before branching on the variable's value. Because sWorkflowStatus is assigned above the check, the true branch runs and the false branch is shown only for completeness.

:PROCEDURE CheckWorkflowStatus;
    :DECLARE sWorkflowStatus, sMessage, bHasWorkflowStatus;

    sWorkflowStatus := "Active";

    bHasWorkflowStatus := IsDefined("sWorkflowStatus");

    /* Use the variable only after confirming it exists;
    :IF bHasWorkflowStatus;
        sMessage := "Workflow status is: " + sWorkflowStatus;
        UsrMes(sMessage);  /* Displays: Workflow status is: Active;
    :ELSE;
        sMessage := "Workflow status variable not defined";
        UsrMes(sMessage);  /* Displays: Workflow status variable not defined;
    :ENDIF;

    :RETURN;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("CheckWorkflowStatus");

docs.title: Report which names from a candidate list are defined
docs.text:
Iterate over a list of candidate variable names and build a comma-separated summary of those currently in scope. The single UsrMes call at the end shows either the list of available names or a not-defined message.

:PROCEDURE SummarizeAvailableInputs;
    :PARAMETERS aCandidateVars;
    :DECLARE nIndex, sVarName, sAvailable, sMessage;

    /* Build a readable list of the names that currently exist;
    sAvailable := "";

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aCandidateVars);
        sVarName := aCandidateVars[nIndex];

        :IF IsDefined(sVarName);
            :IF Empty(sAvailable);
                sAvailable := sVarName;
            :ELSE;
                sAvailable := sAvailable + ", " + sVarName;
            :ENDIF;
        :ENDIF;
    :NEXT;

    :IF Empty(sAvailable);
        sMessage := "No optional input variables are defined";
    :ELSE;
        sMessage := "Available input variables: " + sAvailable;
    :ENDIF;

    UsrMes(sMessage);

    :RETURN sAvailable;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("SummarizeAvailableInputs", {{"sCacheKey", "sContextId"}});

UsrMes displays:

Available input variables: sCacheKey, sContextId

docs.title: Resolve the first non-empty variable from a candidate list
docs.text:
Walk a list of candidate variable names and read the first one that is both defined and non-empty using GetByName. The IsDefined check prevents GetByName from raising an error on a missing name.

:PROCEDURE ResolveBatchIdentifier;
    :PARAMETERS aCandidateVars;
    :DECLARE nIndex, sVarName, vBatchValue, sBatchId;

    sBatchId := "";

    /* Read the first available variable from a list of supported names;
    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aCandidateVars);
        sVarName := aCandidateVars[nIndex];

        :IF IsDefined(sVarName);
            vBatchValue := GetByName(sVarName);

            :IF ! Empty(vBatchValue);
                sBatchId := LimsString(vBatchValue);
                :EXITFOR;
            :ENDIF;
        :ENDIF;
    :NEXT;

    :IF Empty(sBatchId);
        ErrorMes("No batch identifier variable is available");
        :RETURN .F.;
    :ENDIF;

    UsrMes("Using batch identifier: " + sBatchId);
    /* Displays selected batch identifier;

    :RETURN .T.;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ResolveBatchIdentifier", {{"sBatchId", "sOrderNo"}});

docs.title: IsGuid
docs.text:
Validates whether a string matches the GUID format and returns a boolean result.

IsGuid returns .T. when sGuid matches the expected hyphenated GUID pattern and .F. for non-matching or empty strings. The accepted format is 32 hexadecimal characters grouped as 8-4-4-4-12, with optional surrounding braces such as {3F2504E0-4F89-11D3-9A0C-0305E82C3301}. Hex digits are accepted in either uppercase or lowercase. If sGuid is NIL, the function raises an error instead of returning .F..

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When input should contain a GUID and you need to validate it before using it.
  • When filtering imported values into valid and invalid identifier lists.
  • When a value may be missing and you want to guard against the error raised for NIL input.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: IsGuid(sGuid)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description sGuid string yes — String to validate against the GUID format

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: boolean — .T. when sGuid matches the GUID format; otherwise .F.

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text: Trigger Exception message sGuid is NIL. IsGuid (Parameter 'sGuid')

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Validate external identifier strings before using them in lookup or save logic.
  • Guard values that may be NIL before calling IsGuid.
  • Accept both uppercase and lowercase GUID text when the format is otherwise valid.

Don't

  • Assume any non-empty identifier string is valid without checking it.
  • Pass NIL and expect .F. because the function raises an error for missing input.
  • Reject a GUID only because it uses lowercase hex digits or surrounding braces.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Empty strings return .F..
  • The format must include hyphens in the standard 8-4-4-4-12 layout; a 32-character hex string without hyphens is not accepted.

docs.title: Validate a user-provided identifier before use
docs.text:
Call IsGuid on a single input string and branch on the result to confirm whether it is safe to pass to lookup or save logic. The hardcoded value is a valid GUID, so the true branch executes.

:PROCEDURE ValidateGuidInput;
    :DECLARE sUserInput, bIsValid;

    sUserInput := "3F2504E0-4F89-11D3-9A0C-0305E82C3301";

    bIsValid := IsGuid(sUserInput);

    :IF bIsValid;
        UsrMes("Valid GUID: " + sUserInput);
    :ELSE;
        UsrMes("Invalid GUID: " + sUserInput);
    :ENDIF;

    :RETURN bIsValid;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ValidateGuidInput");

UsrMes displays:

Valid GUID: 3F2504E0-4F89-11D3-9A0C-0305E82C3301

docs.title: Filter imported identifiers into valid and invalid groups
docs.text:
Iterate over a mixed list of identifier strings and use IsGuid to route each one into a valid or invalid collection. The summary line shows the final counts for both groups.

:PROCEDURE FilterValidGuids;
    :DECLARE aRawIdentifiers, aValidGuids, aInvalidIds, sIdentifier, nIndex, sReport;

    aRawIdentifiers := {
        "A45E7F92-8B3D-4D9A-9F3C-2B1A6D8E4C71",
        "NOT_A_GUID",
        "12345678-1234-1234-1234-123456789012",
        "INVALID",
        "B72F3C19-9E2A-4D8B-91F7-3C5D8E6A1924",
        "MISSING_HYPHENS",
        "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"
    };

    aValidGuids := {};
    aInvalidIds := {};

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aRawIdentifiers);
        sIdentifier := aRawIdentifiers[nIndex];

        :IF IsGuid(sIdentifier);
            AAdd(aValidGuids, sIdentifier);
        :ELSE;
            AAdd(aInvalidIds, sIdentifier);
        :ENDIF;
    :NEXT;

    sReport := "Valid: " + LimsString(ALen(aValidGuids))
                + " | Invalid: " + LimsString(ALen(aInvalidIds));
    UsrMes(sReport);

    :RETURN aValidGuids;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("FilterValidGuids");

UsrMes displays:

Valid: 4 | Invalid: 3

docs.title: Guard against NIL input with explicit validation and error handling
docs.text:
When IsGuid receives a value from an external source that may be NIL or a non-string type, check for NIL first and convert to string before calling the function. Wrap the call in :TRY/:CATCH for any remaining unexpected errors.

:PROCEDURE ValidateOptionalGuid;
    :PARAMETERS vGuid;
    :DECLARE sGuid, bIsValid, oErr;

    :IF vGuid == NIL;
        UsrMes("No GUID was provided");
        :RETURN .F.;
    :ENDIF;

    sGuid := LimsString(vGuid);

    :TRY;
        bIsValid := IsGuid(sGuid);

        :IF bIsValid;
            UsrMes("Validated GUID: " + sGuid);
        :ELSE;
            UsrMes("Value is not a GUID: " + sGuid);
        :ENDIF;

        :RETURN bIsValid;
    :CATCH;
        oErr := GetLastSSLError();
        ErrorMes("GUID validation failed: " + oErr:Description);
        /* Displays on failure: GUID validation failed;
        :RETURN .F.;
    :ENDTRY;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ValidateOptionalGuid", {"3F2504E0-4F89-11D3-9A0C-0305E82C3301"});

docs.title: IsHex
docs.text:
Validates whether a string contains only uppercase hexadecimal characters.

IsHex returns .T. when sSource contains only the characters 0-9 and A-F. The check is case-sensitive, so lowercase a-f characters return .F.. The function also accepts the empty string, which returns .T. because it contains no non-hex characters. If sSource is NIL, the function raises an error.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When validating input before passing it to other hex-processing logic.
  • When checking whether a value contains only uppercase hexadecimal digits.
  • When normalizing mixed-case input with Upper and then validating it.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: IsHex(sSource)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description sSource string yes — String to validate against the uppercase hexadecimal character set 0-9 and A-F.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: boolean — .T. when sSource contains only uppercase hexadecimal characters; otherwise .F.

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text: Trigger Exception message sSource is NIL. Source string cannot be null.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Check that sSource is not NIL before calling IsHex.
  • Use Upper first when your input may contain lowercase a-f.
  • Add a length check when the next step requires at least one hex digit.
  • Validate untrusted hex input before sending it to downstream conversion logic.

Don't

  • Assume NIL input returns .F.. It raises an error instead.
  • Assume lowercase a-f is accepted. IsHex only accepts uppercase A-F.
  • Skip validation when later logic expects a hex-only string.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Lowercase a-f is rejected. Use Upper first if you want to accept mixed-case input.
  • Leading prefixes such as 0x, whitespace, and punctuation cause the check to fail.
  • The empty string returns .T.. Add a separate length check when at least one hex digit is required before conversion.

docs.title: Check whether a string contains only uppercase hex characters
docs.text:
Call IsHex on a hardcoded value and branch on the result. The input "A3F7" contains only valid uppercase hex digits, so the true branch executes.

:PROCEDURE ValidateHexCode;
    :DECLARE sUserInput, bIsValidHex, sStatus;

    sUserInput := "A3F7";
    bIsValidHex := IsHex(sUserInput);

    :IF bIsValidHex;
        sStatus := "Valid uppercase hex value";
        UsrMes(sStatus);
        :RETURN .T.;
    :ELSE;
        sStatus := "Value contains non-hex characters";
        UsrMes(sStatus);
        :RETURN .F.;
    :ENDIF;
:ENDPROC;

Call with DoProc("ValidateHexCode").

docs.title: Normalize mixed-case input before validating
docs.text:
Trim whitespace and convert to uppercase before calling IsHex, so that values like " a3f7 " pass validation rather than failing on case or whitespace. Calling with "a3f7" produces "A3F7" after normalization, which is valid; calling with "not hex" produces "NOT HEX", which is not.

:PROCEDURE NormalizeAndValidateHex;
    :PARAMETERS sInput;
    :DECLARE sHexValue, bIsValidHex;

    sHexValue := Upper(AllTrim(sInput));
    bIsValidHex := IsHex(sHexValue);

    :IF bIsValidHex;
        UsrMes("Normalized value is valid: " + sHexValue);
        /* Displays normalized valid message;
    :ELSE;
        UsrMes("Normalized value is not valid hex: " + sHexValue);
        /* Displays normalized invalid message;
    :ENDIF;

    :RETURN bIsValidHex;
:ENDPROC;

Call with DoProc("NormalizeAndValidateHex", {"a3f7"}).

docs.title: Filter and convert a batch of hex strings
docs.text:
Use IsHex as a gate before calling LHex2Dec, so that only valid uppercase hex strings are converted. From the five inputs, "ab" (lowercase), "0x10" (prefix), and "FF", "1A", and "7B" (valid) demonstrate how the filter works. Three values pass and two are silently skipped.

:PROCEDURE ConvertValidHexBatch;
    :DECLARE aRawValues, aDecimalValues, sHexValue, nDecimalValue, nIndex;

    aRawValues := {"1A", "FF", "ab", "0x10", "7B"};
    aDecimalValues := {};

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aRawValues);
        sHexValue := aRawValues[nIndex];

        :IF IsHex(sHexValue);
            nDecimalValue := LHex2Dec(sHexValue);
            AAdd(aDecimalValues, nDecimalValue);
        :ENDIF;
    :NEXT;

    :RETURN aDecimalValues;
:ENDPROC;

Call with DoProc("ConvertValidHexBatch").

docs.title: IsInTransaction
docs.text:
Returns .T. if the specified database connection currently has an open transaction.

IsInTransaction checks whether the connection identified by vConnection is currently inside a transaction. vConnection can be a connection name or a SQLConnection object returned by GetConnectionByName. If vConnection is NIL or empty, the function checks the default connection. If the supplied name does not match a known connection, or if the internal database collection is unavailable, an error is raised. This function does not alter, begin, or end transactions.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When your logic must decide between transactional or non-transactional execution depending on active transaction state.
  • When ensuring that a critical operation (e.g., bulk update, audit trail write) only proceeds inside an open transaction.
  • When constructing robust error-handling or compensation routines around transactional operations.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: IsInTransaction([vConnection])

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description vConnection string or SQLConnection no NIL Connection name or connection object specifying which database connection to check. If NIL or empty, the default connection is used.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: boolean — .T. if the specified connection is currently within a transaction; .F. if not.

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text: Trigger Exception message vConnection names an unknown connection. The provider name: <name> not found. The runtime database collection has not been initialized. The internal database collection is null

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Pass explicit, known-good connection names when checking transaction state.
  • Confirm a transaction is active before issuing commit or rollback commands.
  • Use with BeginLimsTransaction and EndLimsTransaction for end-to-end transaction management.

Don't

  • Pass user-supplied or dynamic connection names without validating them first. Invalid names raise errors.
  • Use this function as a transaction manager or depth counter. It checks boolean state only.
  • Assume the function returns .F. for unknown connections. It raises an error instead.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• If vConnection is NIL, empty, or missing, the default connection is used.
  • vConnection can be a connection name or a SQLConnection object returned by GetConnectionByName.
  • Passing an unknown connection name raises an error rather than returning .F..
  • If the internal database collection is unavailable, an error is raised and the function does not return a value.
  • Only tests direct transaction state for the specified connection — does not account for nested or distributed transactions.

docs.title: Guard a commit with a transaction state check
docs.text:
Check the default connection before committing. If a transaction is active, the procedure commits it and reports success; otherwise it reports that no transaction is open.

:PROCEDURE CommitIfActive;
    :DECLARE bInTrans;

    bInTrans := IsInTransaction();

    :IF bInTrans;
        EndLimsTransaction("MyTransaction", .T.);
        UsrMes("Transaction committed successfully");
    :ELSE;
        UsrMes("No active transaction to commit");
    :ENDIF;

    :RETURN bInTrans;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("CommitIfActive");

docs.title: Check a named connection with error handling
docs.text:
Pass an explicit connection name and wrap the call in :TRY/:CATCH to handle unknown connection errors gracefully. The catch block retrieves the error with GetLastSSLError and logs it via ErrorMes.

:PROCEDURE CheckNamedConnectionTransaction;
    :PARAMETERS sConnectionName;
    :DECLARE bInTransaction, oErr;

    :TRY;
        bInTransaction := IsInTransaction(sConnectionName);

        :IF bInTransaction;
            UsrMes(sConnectionName + " has an active transaction");
        :ELSE;
            UsrMes(sConnectionName + " has no active transaction");
        :ENDIF;

        :RETURN bInTransaction;
    :CATCH;
        oErr := GetLastSSLError();
        ErrorMes("Transaction check failed: " + oErr:Description);
        /* Displays on failure: transaction check failed;
        :RETURN .F.;
    :ENDTRY;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("CheckNamedConnectionTransaction", {"LIMS_PROD"});

docs.title: IsInvariantDate
docs.text:
Checks whether a date value has an unspecified (invariant) kind.

IsInvariantDate() returns .T. when dDate has an unspecified DateTimeKind and .F. when it is local or UTC. Passing NIL or a non-date value raises an error.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When you need to distinguish between invariant (unspecified) dates and those with a defined context (local or UTC).
  • When validating input from sources where date kind may affect downstream logic or data integrity.
  • When building workflows that require special handling for uninitialized or default date values.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: IsInvariantDate(dDate)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description dDate date yes — The date value to check for an unspecified DateTimeKind.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: boolean — .T. when dDate has an unspecified DateTimeKind; .F. otherwise.

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text: Trigger Exception message dDate is NIL. Argument: date cannot be null. dDate is not a date value. Argument: date must be of date type

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Always validate that your input is a date before calling this function.
  • Use IsInvariantDate to clearly separate invariant date logic from local and UTC handling.
  • Combine with MakeDateInvariant or MakeDateLocal for comprehensive date workflows.

Don't

  • Assume that user-provided or external values are already of date type — validate before calling to avoid runtime errors.
  • Attempt to infer date kind using property checks or workarounds. This function provides a single-point, reliable check for invariance.
  • Ignore potential default or uninitialized dates in business logic. Unspecified dates can introduce subtle bugs if left unchecked.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text: • This function does not convert or mutate the input value — it only inspects it.

docs.title: Block a workflow when the review date is invariant
docs.text:
Call IsInvariantDate on a date produced by CToD with an empty string. An empty date string yields an invariant date, so the :IF branch fires and ErrorMes blocks the workflow. If a proper date were supplied, the :ELSE branch would fire and InfoMes would display the approval message.

:PROCEDURE CheckReviewDateInvariant;
    :DECLARE dReviewDate, bIsInvariant;

    dReviewDate := CToD("");
    bIsInvariant := IsInvariantDate(dReviewDate);

    :IF bIsInvariant;
        ErrorMes("Workflow blocked: review date is uninitialized");
    :ELSE;
        InfoMes("Workflow approved: review date is properly set");
    :ENDIF;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("CheckReviewDateInvariant");

docs.title: Count user-set versus invariant dates across imported records
docs.text:
Iterate over a list of sample records, each represented as a three-element array of {dReceived, dAnalyzed, dReported} dates, and use IsInvariantDate to count how many date fields are set versus still invariant. With the data below, three fields have real dates and six are invariant, and the warning fires because nInvariantDates is greater than zero.

:PROCEDURE ValidateImportedDates;
    :DECLARE aRecords, aDates;
    :DECLARE nIndex, nDateIndex, nUserDates, nInvariantDates, sMsg;

    nUserDates := 0;
    nInvariantDates := 0;

    aRecords := {
        {CToD("04/08/2026"), CToD(""), CToD("")},
        {CToD("04/07/2026"), CToD("04/08/2026"), CToD("")},
        {CToD(""), CToD(""), CToD("")}
    };

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aRecords);
        aDates := aRecords[nIndex];

        :FOR nDateIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aDates);
            :IF IsInvariantDate(aDates[nDateIndex]);
                nInvariantDates := nInvariantDates + 1;
            :ELSE;
                nUserDates := nUserDates + 1;
            :ENDIF;
        :NEXT;
    :NEXT;

    sMsg := "Imported dates: " + LimsString(nUserDates) + " user-set, "
            + LimsString(nInvariantDates) + " invariant/unset";
    UsrMes(sMsg);

    :IF nInvariantDates > 0;
        sMsg := "Warning: " + LimsString(nInvariantDates) + " date fields require completion";
        UsrMes(sMsg);
    :ENDIF;

    :RETURN .T.;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ValidateImportedDates");

docs.title: IsNumeric
docs.text:
Determines whether a string is a valid numeric value, with optional support for hexadecimal input.

IsNumeric checks whether sNumber can be interpreted as a number using the current STARLIMS decimal and group separator settings. It returns .T. for values that the runtime accepts as floating-point text with optional thousands separators, and .F. for strings that do not match that format. If bAllowHex is .T., the function also accepts whole hexadecimal strings in 0x... or 0X... form.

Leading and trailing whitespace are allowed. Empty strings, NaN, positive infinity, and negative infinity return .F.. Passing NIL or a non-string value raises an error instead of returning .F..

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When you need to validate a string before calling ToNumeric.
  • When imported or user-entered values may include group separators.
  • When hexadecimal input should be accepted only in specific paths.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: IsNumeric(sNumber, [bAllowHex])

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Default Description sNumber string yes — String to validate. bAllowHex boolean no .F. When .T., also accepts whole hexadecimal strings such as 0xFF. Non-boolean values are treated as .F..

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: boolean — .T. when sNumber is a valid decimal value under the current numeric settings, or a valid 0x... or 0X... hexadecimal string when bAllowHex is enabled; otherwise .F..

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text: Trigger Exception message sNumber is NIL or not a string. Value cannot be null. (Parameter 'sNumber')

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use IsNumeric before ToNumeric when invalid input should be handled without exceptions.
  • Pass bAllowHex as .T. only when 0x-prefixed values are valid for that input.
  • Keep user input as a string until validation is complete.

Don't

  • Assume NIL or non-string values return .F.. They raise an error instead.
  • Assume hexadecimal strings are accepted by default. They only pass when bAllowHex is .T..
  • Use IsNumeric when you need the parsed numeric value. Use ToNumeric or Val after validation.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• NaN, positive infinity, and negative infinity return .F..
  • Hexadecimal support is limited to whole values in 0x... or 0X... form.

docs.title: Validate a text field before conversion
docs.text:
Check a user-entered value before converting it to a number. The input "1,234.50" is valid with a thousands separator, so IsNumeric returns .T., the early-return branch is skipped, and ToNumeric converts the string to 1234.5.

:PROCEDURE ValidateNumericInput;
    :DECLARE sUserInput, bIsNumeric, nValue;

    sUserInput := "1,234.50";
    bIsNumeric := IsNumeric(sUserInput);

    :IF !bIsNumeric;
        UsrMes("Enter a valid number.");
        :RETURN .F.;
    :ENDIF;

    nValue := ToNumeric(sUserInput);
    UsrMes("Accepted value: " + LimsString(nValue));
    /* Displays converted numeric value on success;

    :RETURN .T.;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ValidateNumericInput");

docs.title: Distinguish decimal and hexadecimal input
docs.text:
Check the same value against both decimal and hex modes to route it into the correct conversion path. For "0xFF", the decimal check fails and the hex check succeeds, so ToNumeric is called with bAllowHex .T. and returns 255.

:PROCEDURE ParseConfigValue;
    :DECLARE sConfigValue, bIsDecimal, bIsHex, nValue;

    sConfigValue := "0xFF";
    bIsDecimal := IsNumeric(sConfigValue, .F.);
    bIsHex := IsNumeric(sConfigValue, .T.);

    :IF bIsDecimal;
        nValue := ToNumeric(sConfigValue);
        UsrMes("Decimal value: " + LimsString(nValue));
        /* Displays parsed decimal value;
        :RETURN nValue;
    :ENDIF;

    :IF bIsHex;
        nValue := ToNumeric(sConfigValue, .T.);
        UsrMes("Hex value: " + LimsString(nValue));
        /* Displays parsed hexadecimal value;
        :RETURN nValue;
    :ENDIF;

    UsrMes("Config value is not numeric.");

    :RETURN 0;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ParseConfigValue");

docs.title: Filter imported rows and collect invalid IDs for review
docs.text:
Iterate over a batch of imported rows, use IsNumeric to classify each record ID, and collect failures separately for follow-up. The bAllowHex parameter is forwarded so callers can opt in to hex IDs without changing the validation logic.

:PROCEDURE CleanImportedSampleData;
    :PARAMETERS aImportedRows, bAllowHex;
    :DEFAULT bAllowHex, .F.;
    :DECLARE aCleanRows, aInvalidRows, sRecordId, nIndex;

    aCleanRows := {};
    aInvalidRows := {};

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aImportedRows);
        sRecordId := aImportedRows[nIndex, 1];

        :IF IsNumeric(sRecordId, bAllowHex);
            AAdd(aCleanRows, aImportedRows[nIndex]);
        :ELSE;
            AAdd(aInvalidRows, {sRecordId, nIndex});
        :ENDIF;
    :NEXT;

    UsrMes("Valid rows: " + LimsString(ALen(aCleanRows)));
    /* Displays valid row count;
    UsrMes("Invalid rows: " + LimsString(ALen(aInvalidRows)));
    /* Displays invalid row count;

    :RETURN aCleanRows;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("CleanImportedSampleData", {{{"001", "Sample A"}, {"0xFF", "Sample B"}, {"bad_id", "Sample C"}}, .T.});

docs.title: IsProductionModeOn
docs.text:
Returns .T. when the application's production mode flag is enabled and .F. otherwise.

IsProductionModeOn reads the environment configuration set at startup. It takes no parameters and raises no exceptions. Use it to enable stricter behavior, suppress diagnostic output, or restrict features to production environments.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When you need to enforce stricter operations, security rules, or feature flags only in production environments.
  • When logging, diagnostics, or debug utilities should behave differently between production and non-production environments.
  • When displaying UI indicators or restricting user actions based on whether the system is in production mode.
  • When implementing environment-specific code branches where production configuration requires unique handling.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: IsProductionModeOn()

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: This function takes no parameters.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: boolean — .T. if the application is running in production mode; .F. otherwise.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use this function to clearly separate code paths that should only run in production environments.
  • Combine this function with logging or error-handling logic for environments where stricter compliance is necessary.

Don't

  • Rely on it for detailed environment distinctions beyond production vs. non-production. The function provides only a binary .T./.F. value and does not distinguish between various non-production modes.
  • Use it as a substitute for user authentication or license validation logic. Production mode only reflects the application environment, not user rights or licenses.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Returns only production vs. non-production — it does not indicate which specific non-production environment is active (e.g., development, staging).
  • Changes to the production mode status require a configuration update and restart; the result does not reflect real-time changes during a session.
  • This function does not validate license types or features.

docs.title: Show a warning badge only in production
docs.text:
Display a caution message when the environment is production so users know they are not in a safe sandbox. The message fires only when bIsProduction is .T..

:PROCEDURE ShowProductionBadge;
    :DECLARE bIsProduction, sBadgeMessage;

    bIsProduction := IsProductionModeOn();

    :IF bIsProduction;
        sBadgeMessage := "PRODUCTION MODE ACTIVE - Exercise caution";
        UsrMes(sBadgeMessage);
    :ENDIF;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ShowProductionBadge");

docs.title: Suppress debug logging in production
docs.text:
Allow detailed trace output only in non-production environments to protect performance and sensitive data. The log entry fires only when bIsProd is .F..

:PROCEDURE LogIfNotProduction;
    :DECLARE sModule, sMessage, sLogEntry, bIsProd;

    bIsProd := IsProductionModeOn();
    sModule := "DataProcessing";
    sMessage := "Processing batch of 500 records";

    :IF ! bIsProd;
        sLogEntry := "[" + sModule + "] DEBUG: " + sMessage;
        UsrMes(sLogEntry);
    :ENDIF;

    :RETURN;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("LogIfNotProduction");

docs.title: Enforce role-based access in production
docs.text:
Apply stricter role validation in production while allowing any user through in non-production. The procedure returns .T. if access is granted and .F. if it is denied.

:PROCEDURE ValidateApiEndpoint;
    :DECLARE bIsProduction, bAccessGranted, sEndpoint, sUserRole;
    :DECLARE sRequiredRole, sValidationMsg;

    bAccessGranted := .F.;
    sUserRole := "analyst";
    sRequiredRole := "supervisor";
    sEndpoint := "lims.api.samples.submit";

    bIsProduction := IsProductionModeOn();

    :IF bIsProduction;
        :IF sUserRole == sRequiredRole;
            bAccessGranted := .T.;
            sValidationMsg := "Access granted: role verified in production";
        :ELSE;
            bAccessGranted := .F.;
            sValidationMsg := "Access denied: elevated role required in production for " + sEndpoint;
        :ENDIF;
    :ELSE;
        bAccessGranted := .T.;
        sValidationMsg := "Access granted in non-production environment";
    :ENDIF;

    UsrMes(sValidationMsg);  /* Displays access result;

    :RETURN bAccessGranted;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ValidateApiEndpoint");
