docs.title: CLASS
docs.text:
Defines a class in SSL. A class file can declare fields, methods, an optional base class, and a Constructor procedure for instance initialization.

One class per script

A file is either a class file or a script file. A :CLASS definition runs to end-of-file, so each class must live in its own script.

The :CLASS keyword starts a class definition. After :CLASS, you can add an optional :INHERIT line, one or more :DECLARE field declarations, regular methods defined with :PROCEDURE, and an optional Constructor declared as :PROCEDURE Constructor;. If :INHERIT is omitted, the class inherits from the built-in base object automatically. If you omit the constructor, SSL creates an empty zero-argument constructor for you. There is no :ENDCLASS keyword; the class continues to the end of the file.

Use CreateUdObject to instantiate a user-defined class. Inside class methods, call sibling methods with Me:MethodName() and inherited methods with Base:MethodName().

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
:CLASS changes the file into class context. After the class declaration, SSL accepts an optional :INHERIT, then field declarations and method definitions. The class body is not closed by another keyword; it ends only at end-of-file.

Class fields are declared with :DECLARE in the class body and are available to class methods. Methods are defined with :PROCEDURE ... :ENDPROC just like script procedures, but inside a class they are invoked through the instance, for example oSample:GetSummary() or Me:GetSummary().

Constructors use the reserved declaration name Constructor. A constructor may take parameters, but it cannot return a value. If no constructor is declared, SSL generates an empty zero-argument constructor automatically.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When you need to keep related state and behavior together in one reusable type.
  • When several operations should share the same fields across multiple methods.
  • When you want to extend another SSL class with :INHERIT.
  • When object-style code is clearer than passing many separate values between procedures.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
:CLASS ClassName;

:CLASS ClassName;
:INHERIT category.scriptname;

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Description ClassName Identifier No Name of the class. SSL permits :CLASS;, but reviewed code should provide an explicit class name.

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Procedures & Classes Role: opener

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Put :INHERIT immediately after :CLASS when the class derives from another class.
  • Keep class fields in :DECLARE statements near the top of the file so the object shape is easy to read.
  • Place regular methods before Constructor and keep the constructor last for predictable structure.
  • Instantiate user-defined classes with CreateUdObject instead of built-in-class curly-brace syntax.
  • Use Me: for sibling method calls and Base: for inherited behavior inside class methods.

Don't

  • Mix script logic and a class definition in the same file. :CLASS runs to end-of-file, so the file must stay class-only.
  • Use ClassName{} syntax for a user-defined class — that pattern targets built-in class construction rules.
  • Return a value from Constructor. SSL treats that as a compile-time error.
  • Split related fields across method bodies. Declaring them in the class body makes the instance state clear and consistent.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text: • Members whose names start with _ are excluded from reflection-based access.

docs.title: Define a simple class and instantiate it
docs.text:
Create a class with fields, a regular method, and a constructor that receives initial values. GetDescription returns a formatted string built from the instance fields.

Class definition:

:CLASS SampleAnalyzer;

:DECLARE sSampleName, nResultValue;

:PROCEDURE GetDescription;
    :DECLARE sDescription;

    sDescription := "Sample " + sSampleName + ": "
                    + LimsString(nResultValue);

    :RETURN sDescription;
:ENDPROC;

:PROCEDURE Constructor;
    :PARAMETERS sName, nValue;

    sSampleName := sName;
    nResultValue := nValue;
:ENDPROC;

Usage:

:DECLARE oAnalyzer, sSummary;

oAnalyzer := CreateUdObject("Samples.SampleAnalyzer", {"SAM-001", 42});
sSummary := oAnalyzer:GetDescription();

UsrMes(sSummary);

UsrMes displays:

Sample SAM-001: 42

docs.title: Inherit from a base class and extend its behavior
docs.text:
Use :INHERIT in the derived class and call the parent implementation with Base:. The derived GetSummary appends a result value to the base summary.

Base class script:

:CLASS LabSample;

:DECLARE sSampleId, sStatus;

:PROCEDURE GetSummary;
    :RETURN "Sample " + sSampleId + " is " + sStatus;
:ENDPROC;

:PROCEDURE Constructor;
    :PARAMETERS sId, sState;

    sSampleId := sId;
    sStatus := sState;
:ENDPROC;

Derived class script:

:CLASS QcSample;
:INHERIT LabSample;

:DECLARE nResultValue;

:PROCEDURE GetSummary;
    :DECLARE sSummary;

    sSummary := Base:GetSummary();
    sSummary := sSummary + ", result " + LimsString(nResultValue);

    :RETURN sSummary;
:ENDPROC;

:PROCEDURE Constructor;
    :PARAMETERS sId, sState, nResult;

    sSampleId := sId;
    sStatus := sState;
    nResultValue := nResult;
:ENDPROC;

Usage:

:DECLARE oSample;

oSample := CreateUdObject("Samples.QcSample", {"SAM-002", "Logged", 98.6});
UsrMes(oSample:GetSummary());

UsrMes displays:

Sample SAM-002 is Logged, result 98.6

docs.title: Coordinate several class methods through Me:
docs.text:
Use a class to manage state across multiple operations instead of passing many separate values between procedures. LoadAndReport delegates to AddResult via Me: and returns the accumulated summary.

Class definition:

:CLASS ResultTracker;

:DECLARE aValues, nPassCount, nFailCount;

:PROCEDURE AddResult;
    :PARAMETERS nValue, nLimit;

    AAdd(aValues, nValue);

    :IF nValue >= nLimit;
        nPassCount += 1;
    :ELSE;
        nFailCount += 1;
    :ENDIF;
:ENDPROC;

:PROCEDURE GetSummary;
    :DECLARE sSummary;

    sSummary := "Runs: " + LimsString(ALen(aValues));
    sSummary := sSummary + ", pass: " + LimsString(nPassCount);
    sSummary := sSummary + ", fail: " + LimsString(nFailCount);

    :RETURN sSummary;
:ENDPROC;

:PROCEDURE LoadAndReport;
    :PARAMETERS aInputValues, nLimit;
    :DECLARE nIndex;

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aInputValues);
        Me:AddResult(aInputValues[nIndex], nLimit);
    :NEXT;

    :RETURN Me:GetSummary();
:ENDPROC;

:PROCEDURE Constructor;
    aValues := {};
    nPassCount := 0;
    nFailCount := 0;
:ENDPROC;

Usage:

:DECLARE oTracker, sReport;

oTracker := CreateUdObject("Samples.ResultTracker");
sReport := oTracker:LoadAndReport({95, 102, 88, 110}, 90);

UsrMes(sReport);

UsrMes displays:

Runs: 4, pass: 3, fail: 1

docs.text:

  • Me:
  • Base:
  • DECLARE
  • PROCEDURE

docs.title: DECLARE
docs.text:
Declares one or more variables in the current SSL scope.

The :DECLARE keyword adds one or more names to the current scope. In script, procedure, and method bodies, it declares local variables. In a class body, it declares class fields. The declared names are comma-separated identifiers, and each newly created variable starts with the empty string "" as its initial value. :DECLARE is a regular statement, so SSL allows it anywhere a statement is valid, although many teams still group declarations near the top of a routine for readability.

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text: :DECLARE creates storage for each listed name the first time that name is declared in the current runtime scope. The runtime initializes a new declared variable to the empty string, not NIL. Re-declaring the same name is accepted and does not reset the existing runtime value, so a repeated declaration is usually harmless at runtime but still poor style because it obscures where the variable was introduced.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When defining local working variables for a procedure, method, or script.
  • When declaring class fields that should be available to all methods in the class.
  • When you want variables to exist before assignment instead of relying on undeclared-variable warnings or errors.
  • When you want to introduce a new variable at the point where a later block of logic starts using it.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: :DECLARE variable1[, variable2, ...];

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Description variable1[, variable2, ...] Identifier list Yes One or more variable or field names separated by commas.

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Declarations Role: modifier

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Declare variables before their first use.
  • Group related declarations near the start of a routine when that improves readability.
  • Use clear Hungarian-style names for local variables in examples and new code.

Don't

  • Rely on assignment to introduce a variable. SSL expects names to be declared before use.
  • Re-declare the same name casually. SSL accepts it, but repeated declarations make scope harder to follow.
  • Scatter declarations through unrelated logic just because the language permits it. Use later declarations only when they genuinely improve clarity.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• :DECLARE does not assign a custom initial value. If you need one, declare the name and then assign it on a later statement.
  • All colon-prefixed keywords are case-sensitive, so write :DECLARE in uppercase.

docs.title: Defining local variables at procedure start
docs.text:
Declare all working variables before the procedure uses them. With the hardcoded values, the output shows nResult converted to a string via LimsString and concatenated into the message.

:PROCEDURE InitializeSampleRecord;
    :DECLARE sSampleID, sSampleName, nResult, sMessage;

    sSampleID := "LAB-2024-0042";
    sSampleName := "Calcium Carbonate Test";
    nResult := 98.6;

    sMessage := "Sample " + sSampleID + " - " + sSampleName;
    sMessage += " registered with result " + LimsString(nResult);
    UsrMes(sMessage);

    :RETURN sMessage;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("InitializeSampleRecord");

UsrMes displays:

Sample LAB-2024-0042 - Calcium Carbonate Test registered with result 98.6

docs.title: Declaring a variable where a new logic block starts
docs.text:
:DECLARE can appear later in the statement flow when a new working variable becomes needed. Here sAuditText is only declared inside the :IF block where it is used, keeping it scoped to its purpose.

:PROCEDURE DescribeSampleStatus;
    :PARAMETERS sSampleID, bIncludeAudit;
    :DEFAULT bIncludeAudit, .F.;
    :DECLARE sMessage;

    sMessage := "Sample " + sSampleID + " is ready for review";

    :IF bIncludeAudit;
        :DECLARE sAuditText;

        sAuditText := "Checked by " + MYUSERNAME + " on " + DToC(Today());
        sMessage := sMessage + " (" + sAuditText + ")";
    :ENDIF;

    :RETURN sMessage;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("DescribeSampleStatus", {"LAB-001"});

docs.title: Declaring class fields
docs.text:
Use :DECLARE in a class body to define fields shared by all class methods. The fields declared at the class level are readable and writable from every method.

Class definition:

:CLASS SampleContext;

:DECLARE sSampleID, nResultCount;

:PROCEDURE SetSample;
    :PARAMETERS sID;

    sSampleID := sID;
:ENDPROC;

:PROCEDURE AddResult;
    nResultCount += 1;
:ENDPROC;

:PROCEDURE Constructor;
    sSampleID := "";
    nResultCount := 0;
:ENDPROC;

Usage:

:DECLARE oContext, sMessage;

oContext := CreateUdObject("SampleContext");
oContext:SetSample("LAB-2024-0042");
oContext:AddResult();

sMessage := "Tracked sample " + oContext:sSampleID;
sMessage := sMessage + " with " + LimsString(oContext:nResultCount);
sMessage := sMessage + " result";
UsrMes(sMessage);

UsrMes displays:

Tracked sample LAB-2024-0042 with 1 result

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• DEFAULT
  • PUBLIC
  • PARAMETERS

docs.title: DEFAULT
docs.text:
Assigns a fallback expression to a parameter when the caller omits that argument.

The :DEFAULT keyword works with a parameter that was already declared by :PARAMETERS. In standard SSL scripts, procedures, methods, and constructors, each :DEFAULT line must appear immediately after :PARAMETERS, and each line applies to one parameter. The form is :DEFAULT paramName, defaultExpression; with a comma between the parameter name and the expression. When the caller supplies that argument explicitly, SSL keeps the caller's value instead of using the default.

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
:DEFAULT is part of parameter setup, not a general-purpose assignment statement. The referenced name must already be present in the current :PARAMETERS list, and the fallback can be any valid SSL expression. To default multiple parameters, write multiple :DEFAULT lines in sequence.

Info

SSL data source files use different syntax. In data source files, defaults are written inline on the :PARAMETERS line with :=, not as separate :DEFAULT statements.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When a routine has optional trailing parameters with sensible fallback values.
  • When you want older call sites to keep working after adding a new optional parameter.
  • When a common value such as .F., Today(), or a standard status should be applied automatically.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
:PARAMETERS paramName[, otherParam, ...];
:DEFAULT paramName, defaultExpression;

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Required Description paramName Identifier Yes Parameter name to receive the fallback value. It must already appear in the current :PARAMETERS list. defaultExpression Expression Yes Expression evaluated when the caller omits that argument.

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Declarations Role: modifier

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use :DEFAULT for optional parameters that have a clear, stable fallback value.
  • Keep :DEFAULT lines directly under :PARAMETERS so the routine interface is easy to read.
  • Prefer simple fallback expressions that make the omitted-argument behavior obvious.

Don't

  • Use :DEFAULT as a replacement for normal assignments later in the routine. It only belongs in the parameter setup section.
  • Scatter :DEFAULT lines after :DECLARE or executable statements. That placement does not match SSL syntax.
  • Hide important business decisions inside surprising defaults. If a default changes behavior in a meaningful way, document it clearly.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text: • Colon-prefixed keywords are case-sensitive, so write :DEFAULT in uppercase.

docs.title: Defaulting a single optional flag
docs.text:
Use one :DEFAULT line to make a trailing parameter optional. With only sSampleID supplied, bIncludePrefix defaults to .T. and the label is prefixed with "Sample ".

:PROCEDURE FormatSampleLabel;
    :PARAMETERS sSampleID, bIncludePrefix;
    :DEFAULT bIncludePrefix, .T.;
    :DECLARE sLabel;

    sLabel := sSampleID;

    :IF bIncludePrefix;
        sLabel := "Sample " + sLabel;
    :ENDIF;

    InfoMes(sLabel);

    :RETURN sLabel;
:ENDPROC;

/*
Usage:
DoProc("FormatSampleLabel", {"S-001"})
;

InfoMes displays:

Sample S-001

docs.title: Adding optional parameters without changing existing calls
docs.text:
Use multiple :DEFAULT lines so callers can omit new trailing arguments. ShowDefaultedCalls demonstrates both a minimal call and a fully specified call. The row counts depend on the database contents.

:PROCEDURE FetchSampleRows;
    :PARAMETERS sSampleID, sStatus, nMaxRows;
    :DEFAULT sStatus, "Logged";
    :DEFAULT nMaxRows, 25;
    :DECLARE sSQL, aRows;

    sSQL := "
        SELECT sample_id, status, result_value
        FROM sample_result
        WHERE sample_id = ?sSampleID?
          AND status = ?sStatus?
          AND ROWNUM <= ?nMaxRows?
        ORDER BY sample_id
    ";

    aRows := SQLExecute(sSQL);

    :RETURN aRows;
:ENDPROC;

:PROCEDURE ShowDefaultedCalls;
    :DECLARE aDefaultRows, aCustomRows;

    aDefaultRows := DoProc("FetchSampleRows", {"LAB-2024-0042"});
    aCustomRows := DoProc("FetchSampleRows", {"LAB-2024-0042", "Complete", 10});

    /* Displays row counts for the default and custom calls;
    InfoMes("Default call returned " + LimsString(ALen(aDefaultRows)) + " row(s)");
    InfoMes("Custom call returned " + LimsString(ALen(aCustomRows)) + " row(s)");
:ENDPROC;

/*
Usage:
DoProc("ShowDefaultedCalls")
;

docs.title: Combining expression defaults with branch-specific logic
docs.text:
Default values can be expressions. Here MYUSERNAME and Today() provide automatic context. The routine then branches on bIncludeClosed to build the appropriate query.

:PROCEDURE SummarizeUserQueue;
    :PARAMETERS sOwner, dRunDate, bIncludeClosed;
    :DEFAULT sOwner, MYUSERNAME;
    :DEFAULT dRunDate, Today();
    :DEFAULT bIncludeClosed, .F.;
    :DECLARE sSQL, aRows, sMessage;

    :IF bIncludeClosed;
        sSQL := "
            SELECT task_id, status
            FROM task_queue
            WHERE owner = ?sOwner?
              AND run_date <= ?dRunDate?
            ORDER BY task_id
        ";
    :ELSE;
        sSQL := "
            SELECT task_id, status
            FROM task_queue
            WHERE owner = ?sOwner?
              AND run_date <= ?dRunDate?
              AND status != 'Closed'
            ORDER BY task_id
        ";
    :ENDIF;

    aRows := SQLExecute(sSQL);
    sMessage := "Returned " + LimsString(ALen(aRows)) + " task row(s) for " + sOwner;
    /* Displays the returned row count for the selected owner;
    InfoMes(sMessage);

    :RETURN aRows;
:ENDPROC;

/*
Usage:
DoProc("SummarizeUserQueue")
;

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• PARAMETERS
  • DECLARE

docs.title: ELSE
docs.text:
Directs control to an alternate set of statements when the preceding IF condition is false.

ELSE is the optional alternate branch of an :IF ... :ENDIF block. When the :IF condition evaluates to false, execution continues in the :ELSE branch. When the :IF condition evaluates to true, the :ELSE branch is skipped.

:ELSE does not take parameters or an expression. It is only valid between the statements for :IF and the closing :ENDIF, so it cannot be used as a standalone statement or repeated within the same conditional block.

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text: Use :ELSE; after the true branch statements of an :IF block and before :ENDIF;. The :ELSE branch is optional, but when present it defines the statements that run only when the :IF condition is false.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When you need the program to perform one operation if a condition is true, and a different operation if it is false.
  • When you want to ensure there is always a fall-back action if the main condition is not met.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
:ELSE;

Within a full conditional block:

:IF bCondition;
    /* Statements for the true branch;
:ELSE;
    /* Statements for the false branch;
:ENDIF;

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Control Flow Role: separator

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use :ELSE to clearly define mutually exclusive logic paths.
  • Always keep :ELSE branches as minimal as necessary.

Don't

  • Nest :IF blocks in the :ELSE section when simple two-way branching is sufficient. Clarity is improved and branching logic stays concise.
  • Place unrelated logic within :ELSE just to re-use the block. Each branch should address one logical alternative for easier understanding and maintenance.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text: • Keywords in SSL are case-sensitive and must be written in uppercase.

docs.title: Handling alternate workflow when validation fails
docs.text:
:ELSE executes when the :IF condition is not met. With sStatus set to "REJECTED", the condition is false and the :ELSE branch runs.

:PROCEDURE ValidateSampleStatus;
    :DECLARE sStatus, sResult;

    sStatus := "REJECTED";

    :IF sStatus == "APPROVED";
        sResult := "Sample passed validation";
    :ELSE;
        sResult := "Sample failed validation and requires review";
    :ENDIF;

    UsrMes(sResult);

    :RETURN sResult;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ValidateSampleStatus");

UsrMes displays:

Sample failed validation and requires review

docs.title: Ensuring default values when data is missing
docs.text:
Use :ELSE to apply a fallback value when a lookup yields no result. If LSearch returns empty for the given ID, sStatus is set to sDefaultStatus instead.

:PROCEDURE GetSampleStatus;
    :PARAMETERS sSampleID;
    :DECLARE sStatus, sDefaultStatus, sResult;

    sDefaultStatus := "PENDING";

    sResult := LSearch("
        SELECT status
        FROM samples
        WHERE sample_id = ?
    ", "",, {sSampleID});

    :IF !Empty(sResult);
        sStatus := sResult;
    :ELSE;
        sStatus := sDefaultStatus;
    :ENDIF;

    :RETURN sStatus;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("GetSampleStatus", {"S-001"});

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• IF
  • ENDIF

docs.title: ENDCASE
docs.text:
Closes a :BEGINCASE block after its :CASE branches and optional :OTHERWISE branch.

Use :ENDCASE; to terminate a CASE structure and continue execution with the next statement after the block. A valid CASE structure must include at least one :CASE before :ENDCASE;, and :OTHERWISE is optional.

:ENDCASE does not decide whether later branches run. That behavior is controlled by :EXITCASE inside each branch. Without :EXITCASE, later :CASE expressions can still be evaluated before control reaches :ENDCASE.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When closing every :BEGINCASE block — :ENDCASE; is required to terminate the structure.
  • When writing multi-branch dispatch logic and you need to mark where execution resumes after all branches.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: :ENDCASE;

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Control Flow Role: closer

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • End every :BEGINCASE block with a matching :ENDCASE;.
  • Place :ENDCASE; after all :CASE branches and any optional :OTHERWISE branch.
  • Keep :ENDCASE; paired with :EXITCASE in branch bodies when you want single-branch behavior.

Don't

  • Write :ENDCASE; outside a :BEGINCASE block.
  • Omit :ENDCASE; and expect SSL to close the CASE block implicitly.
  • Assume :ENDCASE; alone prevents later :CASE branches from running. Use :EXITCASE for that.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• :OTHERWISE is optional, but if present it must appear before :ENDCASE;.
  • CASE keywords are case-sensitive and must be uppercase.

docs.title: Closing a CASE block and continuing afterward
docs.text:
:ENDCASE; terminates the CASE structure so the procedure can continue with the next statement. With nStatusCode set to 2, the second branch matches and sLabel is set to "In Review".

:PROCEDURE GetSampleStatusLabel;
    :DECLARE nStatusCode, sLabel, sStatus;

    nStatusCode := 2;
    sLabel := "";

    :BEGINCASE;
    :CASE nStatusCode == 1;
        sLabel := "Submitted";
        :EXITCASE;
    :CASE nStatusCode == 2;
        sLabel := "In Review";
        :EXITCASE;
    :CASE nStatusCode == 3;
        sLabel := "Approved";
        :EXITCASE;
    :OTHERWISE;
        sLabel := "Unknown";
        :EXITCASE;
    :ENDCASE;

    sStatus := "Status: " + sLabel;
    UsrMes(sStatus);

    :RETURN sLabel;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("GetSampleStatusLabel");

UsrMes displays:

Status: In Review

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• BEGINCASE
  • CASE
  • OTHERWISE
  • EXITCASE

docs.title: ENDIF
docs.text:
Terminates an :IF block, with or without an :ELSE branch.

:ENDIF closes the conditional structure started by :IF. It appears after the :IF body or after an optional :ELSE body, and execution then continues with the next statement after the conditional block. :ENDIF is only valid as the closer for a matching :IF structure and must be written as the uppercase colon-prefixed keyword :ENDIF;.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text: • When closing any :IF block — :ENDIF; is required for both the simple :IF ... :ENDIF form and the :IF ... :ELSE ... :ENDIF form.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: :ENDIF;

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Control Flow Role: closer

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Always pair each :IF with a matching :ENDIF;, including nested conditionals.
  • Keep the :IF, optional :ELSE, and :ENDIF; visibly aligned so the full block is easy to follow.

Don't

  • Omit :ENDIF; and rely on indentation or spacing to imply the end of the block. SSL requires an explicit closing keyword.
  • Mis-case the keyword as :endif or :EndIf. Colon-prefixed SSL keywords are case-sensitive and must be uppercase.

docs.title: Completing a simple conditional block
docs.text:
:ENDIF; closes the :IF block so execution continues with the next statement. With nTemp set to 72, the condition is false and sMessage keeps its initial value.

:PROCEDURE CheckTemperature;
    :DECLARE nTemp, sMessage;

    nTemp := 72;
    sMessage := "Temperature is normal";

    :IF nTemp > 80;
        sMessage := "Temperature exceeds limit";
    :ENDIF;

    UsrMes(sMessage);
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("CheckTemperature");

UsrMes displays:

Temperature is normal

docs.title: Closing an IF...ELSE block
docs.text:
:ENDIF; is the same closing keyword whether the conditional has only a true branch or both branches. With nScore set to 85, the condition is false and the :ELSE branch runs.

:PROCEDURE ClassifyResult;
    :PARAMETERS nScore;
    :DECLARE sResult;

    :IF nScore >= 90;
        sResult := "Pass";
    :ELSE;
        sResult := "Review";
    :ENDIF;

    UsrMes(sResult);

    :RETURN sResult;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ClassifyResult", {85});

UsrMes displays:

Review

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• IF
  • ELSE

docs.title: ENDINLINECODE
docs.text:
Ends a named inline SSL code block opened by :BEGININLINECODE.

:ENDINLINECODE; closes the inline block started by :BEGININLINECODE Name; or :BEGININLINECODE "Name";. Everything between the opener and :ENDINLINECODE; is the inline block body. That body can later be retrieved with GetInlineCode (). After :ENDINLINECODE;, regular script statements continue normally. The keyword does not take arguments and must be written with its terminating semicolon.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When closing a block that was opened with :BEGININLINECODE.
  • When defining named inline SSL that you plan to retrieve later with GetInlineCode().
  • When you want the following statements in the script to continue after the inline block declaration.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: :ENDINLINECODE;

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Organization Role: closer

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Close every :BEGININLINECODE block explicitly with :ENDINLINECODE;.
  • Put :ENDINLINECODE; on its own line so the block boundary is obvious.
  • Keep the inline body valid SSL so the script compiles cleanly.

Don't

  • Omit :ENDINLINECODE; — the inline block must be closed explicitly.
  • Use :ENDINLINECODE; without a matching :BEGININLINECODE — the script will not compile.
  • Treat :ENDINLINECODE; as an executable statement with arguments. It only closes the inline block.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• The block body before :ENDINLINECODE; must be valid SSL.
  • Use REGION instead when you need stored text that is not validated as executable SSL.

docs.title: Close an inline block before retrieving it
docs.text:
Defines a named inline block that calculates a price, closes it with :ENDINLINECODE;, then retrieves and executes the stored code. With nUnitPrice set to 25 and nQuantity to 4, the inline block returns 100.

:PROCEDURE CalculateTotalPrice;
    :DECLARE sInlineCode, nTotal;

    :BEGININLINECODE "CalculateTotal";
        :DECLARE nUnitPrice, nQuantity;

        nUnitPrice := 25;
        nQuantity := 4;

        :RETURN nUnitPrice * nQuantity;
    :ENDINLINECODE;

    sInlineCode := GetInlineCode("CalculateTotal");
    nTotal := ExecUdf(sInlineCode,, .F.);

    DeleteInlineCode("CalculateTotal");

    UsrMes("Total price: " + LimsString(nTotal));

    :RETURN nTotal;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("CalculateTotalPrice");

UsrMes displays:

Total price: 100

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• BEGININLINECODE
  • GetInlineCode
  • DeleteInlineCode

docs.title: ENDPROC
docs.text:
Terminates a procedure block and signals the end of its executable statements.

The :ENDPROC keyword closes a procedure that started with :PROCEDURE. Every valid procedure definition must be explicitly terminated with :ENDPROC;. Reaching :ENDPROC ends the procedure if execution has not already left earlier through :RETURN.

:ENDPROC is structural rather than expressive: it does not take arguments and does not return a value. Use :RETURN when you need to leave a procedure early or send a result back to the caller.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text: • When closing every :PROCEDURE block — :ENDPROC; is required to terminate the definition.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: :ENDPROC;

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Procedures & Classes Role: closer

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • End every :PROCEDURE block with :ENDPROC;.
  • Use :RETURN for early exits or returned values.
  • Keep procedure boundaries visually clear with consistent indentation and spacing.

Don't

  • Omit :ENDPROC; or rely on an implicit end to the procedure.
  • Use :ENDPROC as a substitute for :RETURN when you need to exit early.
  • Place extra procedure-body statements after the closing :ENDPROC;.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Constructors also end with :ENDPROC; because they are declared with :PROCEDURE Constructor; syntax.
  • SSL keywords are case-sensitive, so :endproc is not valid syntax.

docs.title: Closing a procedure after normal execution
docs.text:
:ENDPROC; closes the procedure after all statements have run. The procedure builds a status message and displays it before reaching its natural end.

:PROCEDURE ShowLabStatus;
    :DECLARE sStatus, sMessage;

    sStatus := "Operational";
    sMessage := "Laboratory status: " + sStatus;

    UsrMes(sMessage);
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ShowLabStatus");

UsrMes displays:

Laboratory status: Operational

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• PROCEDURE
  • RETURN
  • CLASS
  • PARAMETERS

docs.title: ENDREGION
docs.text:
Keyword that marks the end of a :REGION block; it has no standalone runtime behavior.

:ENDREGION; closes a preceding :REGION block. SSL stores the text between :REGION name; and :ENDREGION; as region content, so :ENDREGION; is not used as an independent executable statement. It accepts no arguments, returns no value, and has no side effects of its own.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text: • When closing every :REGION block — :ENDREGION; is required to complete the region capture.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: :ENDREGION;

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Organization Role: closer

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Close every :REGION block with a matching :ENDREGION; before returning to normal script statements.
  • Use a valid identifier in the opening :REGION name so the region can be retrieved reliably with GetRegion().

Don't

  • Treat :ENDREGION; as executable logic. It only closes a region capture started by :REGION.
  • Use :REGION and :ENDREGION when you only want editor folding. Prefer /* region and /* endregion comments for code organization.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text: • SSL keywords are case-sensitive, so :endregion is not valid syntax.

docs.title: Closing a named region template
docs.text:
:ENDREGION; ends the region capture so the template text can be retrieved with GetRegion(). The %USER% placeholder is replaced with the current MYUSERNAME value at retrieval time.

:PROCEDURE BuildHeaderTemplate;
    :DECLARE aSource, aTarget, sHeader;

    aSource := {"%USER%"};
    aTarget := {MYUSERNAME};

    /* Capture a reusable text template;
    :REGION ReportHeader;
Laboratory report
Prepared for: %USER%
    :ENDREGION;

    sHeader := GetRegion("ReportHeader", aSource, aTarget);
    UsrMes(sHeader);

    :RETURN sHeader;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("BuildHeaderTemplate");

UsrMes displays this output when the current user is jsmith:

Laboratory report
Prepared for: jsmith

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• REGION
  • GetRegion

docs.title: ENDTRY
docs.text:
Closes a structured :TRY block after its :CATCH and/or :FINALLY sections.

:ENDTRY terminates a structured error-handling block that starts with :TRY. In SSL, a :TRY block must contain at least one statement and must be followed by :CATCH, :FINALLY, or both before the closing :ENDTRY;. When execution reaches :ENDTRY, the protected block is complete and normal statement flow continues with the next statement after the block.

:ENDTRY does not take parameters or expressions. It is only valid as the closing keyword for a :TRY structure, after the optional :CATCH section and the optional :FINALLY section. Using :ENDTRY outside that structure, omitting it, or placing it before the required sections is invalid.

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
:ENDTRY closes these valid structured error-handling forms:

:TRY;
    /* statements;
:CATCH;
    /* error handling;
:ENDTRY;

:TRY;
    /* statements;
:FINALLY;
    /* cleanup;
:ENDTRY;

:TRY;
    /* statements;
:CATCH;
    /* error handling;
:FINALLY;
    /* cleanup;
:ENDTRY;

After :ENDTRY;, execution resumes with the next statement unless control flow was already redirected inside the block, for example by :RETURN; in the :TRY or :CATCH body. When a :FINALLY block is present, it runs before control leaves the structure.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text: • When closing every :TRY block — :ENDTRY; is required to terminate the error-handling structure.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: :ENDTRY;

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Error Handling Role: closer

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Always include :ENDTRY whenever you create a :TRY block, regardless of whether you use :CATCH or :FINALLY sections.
  • Place :ENDTRY only after all associated :CATCH and :FINALLY sections.
  • Format :ENDTRY clearly and align it with its opening :TRY for readability.

Don't

  • Omit :ENDTRY, expecting the block to close automatically. Omitting :ENDTRY causes compile-time errors and leaves error handling incomplete.
  • Insert :ENDTRY before these sections or outside the :TRY block. Early or misplaced :ENDTRY disrupts the intended flow and terminates exception handling prematurely.
  • Scatter related error-handling keywords, making the block hard to follow. Consistent structure improves code clarity and maintainability.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text: • :ENDTRY must be written in uppercase as a colon-prefixed keyword.

docs.title: Closing a TRY/CATCH block and continuing afterward
docs.text:
:ENDTRY; closes the error-handling structure so execution continues with the next statement. When the file read succeeds, a success message displays; when it fails, the :CATCH block handles the error. In both cases, the statement after :ENDTRY; runs.

:PROCEDURE LoadConfigFile;
    :PARAMETERS sFilePath;
    :DECLARE sFileText, oErr;

    :TRY;
        sFileText := ReadText(sFilePath);
        UsrMes("Configuration file loaded");

    :CATCH;
        oErr := GetLastSSLError();
        ErrorMes("Unable to load configuration file: " + oErr:Description);
        /* Displays on failure: unable to load the configuration file;

    :ENDTRY;

    UsrMes("Continuing after the TRY block");
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("LoadConfigFile", {"config.ini"});

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• TRY
  • CATCH
  • FINALLY

docs.title: ENDWHILE
docs.text:
Closes a :WHILE loop block.

:ENDWHILE marks the end of the statements governed by a preceding :WHILE. When execution reaches the end of the loop body, control returns to the :WHILE condition for the next test. If the condition is still true, another iteration starts. If the condition is false, execution continues with the next statement after :ENDWHILE;.

:ENDWHILE does not take parameters or expressions. It is only used as the closing keyword for a :WHILE block.

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
:ENDWHILE closes this loop form:

:WHILE condition;
    /* loop body;
:ENDWHILE;

Only statements between :WHILE and :ENDWHILE belong to the loop body. Statements after :ENDWHILE; run only after the loop finishes.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When you need to close a :WHILE loop explicitly.
  • When you want the loop body boundaries to be clear in nested or longer logic.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: :ENDWHILE;

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Loops Role: closer

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Pair each :ENDWHILE with the :WHILE it closes.
  • Keep repeated work inside the loop body, before :ENDWHILE;.
  • Align :ENDWHILE with its matching :WHILE to make nesting clear.

Don't

  • Treat :ENDWHILE as a standalone statement unrelated to a :WHILE block. It is a structural closer, not an independent control-flow action.
  • Place statements after :ENDWHILE; if they must run on every iteration. Code after the closer runs only after the loop exits.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• :ENDWHILE closes a :WHILE block; it is not used for :FOR loops.
  • :ENDWHILE must be written as an uppercase colon-prefixed keyword.

docs.title: Closing a countdown loop
docs.text:
:ENDWHILE; marks the end of the loop body. On each iteration the counter is decremented and a message displays; when nCount reaches 0 the loop exits and "Done" prints.

:PROCEDURE Countdown;
    :DECLARE nCount, sMessage;

    nCount := 5;

    :WHILE nCount > 0;
        sMessage := "Countdown: " + LimsString(nCount);
        UsrMes(sMessage);
        /* Displays countdown values while nCount is greater than zero;
        nCount := nCount - 1;
    :ENDWHILE;

    UsrMes("Done");
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("Countdown");

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• WHILE
  • EXITWHILE

docs.title: ERROR
docs.text:
Defines a legacy error handler for the statements that follow it in the current procedure or method.

:ERROR starts the legacy scope-based error-handling pattern. Its body must contain at least one statement, and it handles runtime errors raised by subsequent statements in the same scope. When used together with :RESUME, execution can continue after a handled failure. Without :RESUME, the handler still runs, but execution does not resume statement-by-statement.

Use :ERROR primarily when maintaining older SSL code that already relies on the :ERROR / :RESUME pattern. For new code, prefer structured :TRY / :CATCH / :FINALLY blocks.

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
:ERROR applies to the subsequent statements in the current scope, not to a :TRY block.

If a handled statement fails, the :ERROR body runs. Use GetLastSSLError() inside the handler to retrieve the current error object and read properties such as :Description.

If the procedure includes :RESUME, the runtime switches to resume-mode handling so execution can continue after a failing statement. If there is no :RESUME, the handler still catches the error, but the procedure does not continue in that statement-by-statement recovery mode.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When maintaining legacy procedures that already use :ERROR / :RESUME.
  • When you need one shared handler for the remaining statements in the current scope.
  • When recoverable failures should be logged or corrected before optionally continuing with :RESUME.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
:ERROR;

:ERROR is followed by one or more handler statements. In practice it is usually paired with :RESUME; later in the same handler block when the procedure should continue after an error.

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Error Handling Role: handler

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use :ERROR only for legacy handler flows that genuinely need :ERROR / :RESUME.
  • Put the :ERROR block before the statements it is meant to protect.
  • Use GetLastSSLError() and oErr:Description inside the handler when you need a readable error message.
  • Prefer :TRY / :CATCH / :FINALLY for new code.

Don't

  • Describe :ERROR as part of :TRY / :CATCH / :FINALLY. It is a separate legacy mechanism.
  • Use :ERROR for new structured exception-handling examples when :TRY / :CATCH would be clearer.
  • Assume :RESUME is optional when the procedure is meant to continue after failures. Without it, the handler does not provide resume-mode recovery.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• :ERROR is legacy scope-based handling for the current procedure or method. It is not a clause inside :TRY.
  • :RESUME changes the flow to resume-mode handling for subsequent statements. Use it only when continuing is safe.
  • If no handled error occurs, the :ERROR body is skipped.

docs.title: Logging a failure and stopping the procedure
docs.text:
Uses one shared legacy handler to catch any failure in the remaining procedure body. If ReadText fails, the handler logs the error via ErrorMes. If it succeeds, the file content is reported via UsrMes.

:PROCEDURE LoadConfiguration;
    :DECLARE sFilePath, sContent, oErr;

    sFilePath := "config/settings.txt";

    /* Handle any later failure in this procedure;
    :ERROR;
        oErr := GetLastSSLError();
        ErrorMes("Configuration load failed: " + oErr:Description);
        /* Displays on failure: configuration load failed;

    /* If ReadText fails, control jumps to the handler;
    sContent := ReadText(sFilePath);

    /* Only runs when the file was read successfully;
    UsrMes("File loaded successfully: " + sContent);
    /* Displays on success: loaded file contents;

    :RETURN sContent;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("LoadConfiguration");

docs.title: Resuming after per-record failures
docs.text:
Uses :RESUME so a batch loop can continue after logging a recoverable error for a single record. With the input {"S-001", "BAD", "S-003"}, the "BAD" record triggers the error handler and increments the failure count; the other records are processed normally.

:PROCEDURE ImportResults;
    :PARAMETERS aSampleIDs;
    :DECLARE nIndex, sSampleID, oErr, nImported, nFailed;

    nImported := 0;
    nFailed := 0;

    /* Log the failure and continue with the next statement;
    :ERROR;
        oErr := GetLastSSLError();
        nFailed := nFailed + 1;
        UsrMes("Skipping sample " + sSampleID + ": " + oErr:Description);
        /* Displays on failure: skipping the current sample;
    :RESUME;

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aSampleIDs);
        sSampleID := aSampleIDs[nIndex];

        /* Simulate a failing record in the batch;
        :IF sSampleID == "BAD";
            RaiseError("Sample is not valid for import");
        :ENDIF;

        nImported := nImported + 1;
    :NEXT;

    UsrMes(
        "Imported " + LimsString(nImported)
        + " samples. Failed: " + LimsString(nFailed)
    );
    /* Displays a summary of imported and failed records;

    :RETURN nImported;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ImportResults", {{"S-001", "BAD", "S-003"}});

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• RESUME
  • TRY
  • CATCH
  • FINALLY
  • ENDTRY

docs.title: EXITCASE
docs.text:
Ends the current :CASE or :OTHERWISE branch and continues with the first statement after :ENDCASE ;.

Use :EXITCASE; inside a :BEGINCASE block when a matching branch should stop the whole block immediately. When :EXITCASE; is present, execution continues with the statement after :ENDCASE ;. Without it, later :CASE expressions can still be evaluated and additional matching case bodies can run.

:EXITCASE does not take parameters and does not return a value. It is valid only inside a :CASE or :OTHERWISE branch within a :BEGINCASE block.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When one matching branch should prevent all later branches from being evaluated.
  • When you want a :BEGINCASE block to behave like an either-or selection.
  • When you want the control-flow stop to be explicit instead of relying on the reader to reason about fall-through.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: :EXITCASE;

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Control Flow Role: modifier

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • End each :CASE and :OTHERWISE branch with :EXITCASE; unless you intentionally want later matching branches to run.
  • Use :EXITCASE; to make single-branch :BEGINCASE behavior explicit.
  • Keep :EXITCASE; on its own line at the end of the branch body.

Don't

  • Omit :EXITCASE; by accident. Without it, later :CASE expressions are still evaluated and more than one branch body may run.
  • Place statements after :EXITCASE; in the same branch. That code is unreachable and makes the block harder to read.
  • Use :EXITCASE; outside a :BEGINCASE structure.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text: • SSL keywords are case-sensitive. Write :EXITCASE in uppercase.

docs.title: Stop after the first matching branch
docs.text:
:EXITCASE; prevents later branches from running after the current match. With sStatus set to "HIGH", only the second branch executes and the procedure reports its priority message.

:PROCEDURE GetSamplePriority;
    :PARAMETERS sStatus;
    :DECLARE sPriority, sMessage;

    sPriority := "";

    :BEGINCASE;
    :CASE sStatus == "CRITICAL";
        sPriority := "Immediate attention required";
        :EXITCASE;
    :CASE sStatus == "HIGH";
        sPriority := "Process within 24 hours";
        :EXITCASE;
    :CASE sStatus == "STANDARD";
        sPriority := "Queue for normal processing";
        :EXITCASE;
    :OTHERWISE;
        sPriority := "Manual review required";
        :EXITCASE;
    :ENDCASE;

    sMessage := "Sample priority: " + sPriority;
    UsrMes(sMessage);

    :RETURN sPriority;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("GetSamplePriority", {"HIGH"});

UsrMes displays:

Sample priority: Process within 24 hours

docs.title: Classify a result value with exclusive branches
docs.text:
Each branch uses :EXITCASE; so only one category applies. With nResultValue set to 80, the second branch matches (80 >= 75) and the first is skipped (80 < 90).

:PROCEDURE ClassifyResult;
    :PARAMETERS nResultValue;
    :DECLARE sCategory;

    sCategory := "";

    :BEGINCASE;
    :CASE nResultValue >= 90;
        sCategory := "Critical";
        :EXITCASE;
    :CASE nResultValue >= 75;
        sCategory := "Warning";
        :EXITCASE;
    :OTHERWISE;
        sCategory := "Normal";
        :EXITCASE;
    :ENDCASE;

    UsrMes(sCategory);

    :RETURN sCategory;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ClassifyResult", {80});

UsrMes displays:

Warning

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• BEGINCASE
  • CASE
  • OTHERWISE
  • ENDCASE

docs.title: EXITFOR
docs.text:
Terminates the innermost active :FOR loop immediately and continues with the statement after the matching :NEXT.

Use :EXITFOR; when a counted loop should stop early after a condition is met, such as finding the first matching row or detecting a stopping state. Control leaves the current :FOR body immediately, so later statements in that iteration do not run. In nested loops, :EXITFOR affects only the nearest enclosing :FOR loop.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When a counted loop should stop early after finding a target value or meeting a condition.
  • When you want to avoid processing remaining items once the result is known.
  • When only the innermost :FOR loop should exit, leaving outer loops running.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: :EXITFOR;

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Loops Role: modifier

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use :EXITFOR to stop a :FOR loop once the target condition is met.
  • Keep the exit condition explicit with :IF so the early exit is easy to read.
  • Preserve any state the caller needs after the loop, such as a found flag or result value.

Don't

  • Use :EXITFOR outside a :FOR loop. That raises Found :EXITFOR outside :FOR.
  • Place :EXITFOR in a :FINALLY block. That raises Cannot have :EXITFOR inside :FINALLY.
  • Use :EXITFOR as a substitute for ordinary branching when the loop should continue running.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• :EXITFOR must be written in uppercase.
  • Outside a :FOR loop, :EXITFOR raises Found :EXITFOR outside :FOR.
  • Inside a :FINALLY block, :EXITFOR raises Cannot have :EXITFOR inside :FINALLY.

docs.title: Finding the first matching record in a list
docs.text:
Stops iterating through an array after the first active sample is found. With the hardcoded data, "Batch B" at index 2 is the first active sample.

:PROCEDURE FindFirstActiveSample;
    :DECLARE aSamples, nIndex, nSampleId, sSampleName, sStatus, sFoundMsg;

    /* Simulated sample rows;
    aSamples := {
        {1001, "Batch A", "INACTIVE"},
        {1002, "Batch B", "ACTIVE"},
        {1003, "Batch C", "ACTIVE"},
        {1004, "Batch D", "INACTIVE"}
    };

    sFoundMsg := "No active sample found";

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO ALen(aSamples);
        nSampleId := aSamples[nIndex, 1];
        sSampleName := aSamples[nIndex, 2];
        sStatus := aSamples[nIndex, 3];

        :IF sStatus == "ACTIVE";
            sFoundMsg := "Found " + sSampleName
                + " (ID " + LimsString(nSampleId) + ")";
            :EXITFOR;
        :ENDIF;
    :NEXT;

    UsrMes(sFoundMsg);

    :RETURN sFoundMsg;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("FindFirstActiveSample");

UsrMes displays:

Found Batch B (ID 1002)

docs.title: Exit only the inner loop in nested FOR blocks
docs.text:
:EXITFOR exits only the innermost :FOR loop. A bFound flag signals the outer loop to exit on the next iteration. With sTargetID set to "SMP-005", the match is at row 2, column 2.

:PROCEDURE FindSampleInGrid;
    :DECLARE aSampleGrid, sTargetID, sFoundPos, nOuter, nInner, bFound;

    aSampleGrid := {
        {"SMP-001", "SMP-002", "SMP-003"},
        {"SMP-004", "SMP-005", "SMP-006"},
        {"SMP-007", "SMP-008", "SMP-009"}
    };
    sTargetID := "SMP-005";
    sFoundPos := "Not found";
    bFound := .F.;

    /* Search grid and stop inner loop at the first match;
    :FOR nOuter := 1 :TO 3;
        :FOR nInner := 1 :TO 3;
            :IF aSampleGrid[nOuter, nInner] == sTargetID;
                sFoundPos := "Row " + LimsString(nOuter) + ", Col " + LimsString(nInner);
                bFound := .T.;
                :EXITFOR;
            :ENDIF;
        :NEXT;

        :IF bFound;
            :EXITFOR;
        :ENDIF;
    :NEXT;

    UsrMes("Target: " + sTargetID + " -> " + sFoundPos);

    :RETURN bFound;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("FindSampleInGrid");

UsrMes displays:

Target: SMP-005 -> Row 2, Col 2

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• FOR
  • NEXT
  • LOOP
  • EXITWHILE

docs.title: EXITWHILE
docs.text:
Terminates the innermost active :WHILE loop and continues with the statement after the matching :ENDWHILE.

Use :EXITWHILE; when a :WHILE loop should stop as soon as a condition is met, such as finding a match, detecting a fatal validation problem, or reaching a stopping state that makes more iterations unnecessary. Control leaves the current :WHILE body immediately, so later statements in that iteration do not run. In nested loops, :EXITWHILE affects only the nearest enclosing :WHILE.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When a :WHILE loop should stop as soon as a target condition is satisfied.
  • When a critical condition inside the loop means the remaining iterations should be skipped.
  • When an early exit makes the loop clearer than adding more nested :IF blocks.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: :EXITWHILE;

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Loops Role: modifier

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use :EXITWHILE to stop a :WHILE loop once the loop's goal has been reached.
  • Keep the exit condition explicit with :IF so the early exit is easy to follow.
  • Preserve any state the caller needs after the loop, such as a found flag or error message.

Don't

  • Use :EXITWHILE outside a :WHILE loop. That raises Found :EXITWHILE outside :WHILE.
  • Place :EXITWHILE in a :FINALLY block. That raises Cannot have :EXITWHILE inside :FINALLY.
  • Use :EXITWHILE as a substitute for ordinary loop conditions when the loop should exit naturally.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• :EXITWHILE must be written as an uppercase colon-prefixed keyword.
  • Outside a :WHILE loop, :EXITWHILE raises Found :EXITWHILE outside :WHILE.
  • Inside a :FINALLY block, :EXITWHILE raises Cannot have :EXITWHILE inside :FINALLY.

docs.title: Stop validation at the first critical error
docs.text:
Exits the :WHILE loop as soon as a missing sample ID is detected. The hardcoded batch has an empty entry at position 4, so validation halts there and returns .F..

:PROCEDURE ValidateSampleBatch;
    :DECLARE aSamples, sSampleID, nCount, nIndex, bCriticalError, sErrorMsg;

    aSamples := {"SA-1001", "SA-1002", "SA-1003", "", "SA-1005"};
    nCount := ALen(aSamples);
    nIndex := 1;
    bCriticalError := .F.;
    sErrorMsg := "";

    :WHILE nIndex <= nCount;
        sSampleID := aSamples[nIndex];

        :IF Empty(sSampleID);
            bCriticalError := .T.;
            sErrorMsg := "Sample ID is required at position " + LimsString(nIndex);
            ErrorMes(sErrorMsg);  /* Displays on failure: missing sample ID message;
            :EXITWHILE;
        :ENDIF;

        UsrMes("Validated: " + sSampleID);
        nIndex := nIndex + 1;
    :ENDWHILE;

    :IF bCriticalError;
        :RETURN .F.;
    :ENDIF;

    UsrMes("Batch validation complete");

    :RETURN .T.;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ValidateSampleBatch");

docs.title: Exit only the inner WHILE in nested loops
docs.text:
:EXITWHILE exits only the nearest :WHILE. A bFound flag signals the outer loop to exit on the next check. With sTargetID set to "SMP-005", the match is at row 2, column 2.

:PROCEDURE FindTargetCell;
    :DECLARE aSampleGrid, sTargetID, sFoundPos, nRow, nCol, bFound;

    aSampleGrid := {
        {"SMP-001", "SMP-002", "SMP-003"},
        {"SMP-004", "SMP-005", "SMP-006"},
        {"SMP-007", "SMP-008", "SMP-009"}
    };
    sTargetID := "SMP-005";
    sFoundPos := "Not found";
    nRow := 1;
    bFound := .F.;

    :WHILE nRow <= ALen(aSampleGrid);
        nCol := 1;

        :WHILE nCol <= ALen(aSampleGrid[nRow]);
            :IF aSampleGrid[nRow, nCol] == sTargetID;
                sFoundPos := "Row " + LimsString(nRow) + ", Col " + LimsString(nCol);
                bFound := .T.;
                :EXITWHILE;
            :ENDIF;

            nCol := nCol + 1;
        :ENDWHILE;

        :IF bFound;
            :EXITWHILE;
        :ENDIF;

        nRow := nRow + 1;
    :ENDWHILE;

    UsrMes(sFoundPos);

    :RETURN sFoundPos;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("FindTargetCell");

UsrMes displays:

Row 2, Col 2

docs.text:

  • ENDWHILE
  • EXITFOR
  • LOOP

docs.title: FINALLY
docs.text:
Starts the cleanup section of a :TRY block and always runs after the protected work completes.

:FINALLY introduces the final section of a structured :TRY block. The statements in :FINALLY run after the :TRY body finishes, whether the protected work succeeds or raises an error. When a :CATCH block is present, it runs before :FINALLY; when no :CATCH is present, :FINALLY can still be used by itself as the cleanup section before :ENDTRY;.

A :TRY body must contain at least one statement before :FINALLY, and the :FINALLY block itself must also contain at least one statement. :TRY cannot end directly with :ENDTRY; because the structure must include :CATCH, :FINALLY, or both.

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
:FINALLY is part of these valid structured forms:

:TRY;
    try_statements;
:FINALLY;
    cleanup_statements;
:ENDTRY;

:TRY;
    try_statements;
:CATCH;
    catch_statements;
:FINALLY;
    cleanup_statements;
:ENDTRY;

Control-flow statements that would bypass the guaranteed cleanup path are invalid inside :FINALLY. These forms raise compile-time errors:

  • Cannot have :RETURN inside :FINALLY.
  • Cannot have :EXITWHILE inside :FINALLY.
  • Cannot have :EXITFOR inside :FINALLY.
  • Cannot have :LOOP inside :FINALLY.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When cleanup must happen regardless of whether the :TRY body succeeds or fails.
  • When resetting state, releasing locks, or restoring temporary settings after protected work.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: :FINALLY;

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Error Handling Role: modifier

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use :FINALLY for cleanup that must happen whether the :TRY body succeeds or fails.
  • Keep :FINALLY focused on cleanup, state restoration, and other mandatory end-of-block work.
  • Put error-specific recovery in :CATCH and shared cleanup in :FINALLY when you need both.

Don't

  • Put :RETURN, :EXITWHILE, :EXITFOR, or :LOOP inside :FINALLY. All four forms are invalid there.
  • Move main business logic into :FINALLY. That block should stay predictable and cleanup-oriented.
  • Leave a :FINALLY body empty. It must contain at least one statement.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• :FINALLY is only valid inside a :TRY structure.
  • :FINALLY must appear after the :TRY body and before :ENDTRY;.
  • When both blocks are present, :CATCH must come before :FINALLY.
  • Keywords are case-sensitive and must be written in uppercase.

docs.title: Releasing cleanup state after success or failure
docs.text:
Uses :FINALLY to reset a flag that must always be cleared, even when the batch query raises an error. The cleanup runs unconditionally; the :CATCH block only runs when an error occurs.

:PROCEDURE ProcessBatch;
    :PARAMETERS sBatchID;
    :DECLARE aRows, bCleanupNeeded, oErr, sStatus;

    bCleanupNeeded := .F.;
    sStatus := "Starting";

    :TRY;
        /* Mark temporary state that must always be cleared;
        bCleanupNeeded := .T.;
        sStatus := "Loading batch";

        aRows := SQLExecute("
            SELECT sample_id
            FROM sample
            WHERE batch_id = ?sBatchID?
        ");

        :IF ALen(aRows) == 0;
            RaiseError("No samples found for batch " + sBatchID);
        :ENDIF;

        sStatus := "Loaded " + LimsString(ALen(aRows)) + " sample(s)";

    :CATCH;
        oErr := GetLastSSLError();
        sStatus := "Batch load failed: " + oErr:Description;
        UsrMes(sStatus);  /* Displays batch load failure on error;

    :FINALLY;
        :IF bCleanupNeeded;
            bCleanupNeeded := .F.;
            UsrMes("Cleanup complete");
        :ENDIF;

    :ENDTRY;

    :RETURN sStatus;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ProcessBatch", {"BATCH-001"});

docs.title: Related

• TRY
  • CATCH

docs.title: FOR
docs.text:
Executes a counted loop by assigning a numeric variable, checking it against a numeric limit, and updating it after each iteration.

The :FOR keyword defines a counted loop. It initializes a numeric loop variable, checks whether the current value is still within the loop limit, runs the loop body, then updates the variable by the step value before testing again. :STEP is optional and defaults to 1. A :FOR loop always closes with :NEXT;, and :EXITFOR; can leave the loop early.

The limit expression and optional step expression are evaluated once before the loop starts. Changing the variables used in those expressions inside the loop body does not change the current loop's bounds.

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
:FOR runs in this order:

  1. Assign the start value to the loop variable.
  2. Check whether the current value is still within the loop limit.
  3. Run the loop body.
  4. Add the step value to the loop variable.
  5. Repeat until the exit condition fails, then continue after :NEXT;.

When the step is zero or positive, the loop continues while the current value is less than or equal to the limit. When the step is negative, the loop continues while the current value is greater than or equal to the limit.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text:
• When you need to execute a block of statements a known number of times.
  • When iterating through arrays with an explicit numeric index.
  • When counting upward or downward by a fixed increment.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: :FOR nIndex := nStart :TO nEnd [:STEP nStep];

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Description nIndex Numeric loop variable assigned before the first iteration and updated after each iteration. nStart Initial numeric value assigned to the loop variable. nEnd Numeric loop limit checked before each iteration. nStep Optional numeric increment or decrement. If omitted, SSL uses 1.

docs.title: Keyword group
docs.text: Group: Loops Role: opener

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Do

  • Use :STEP only when you need an increment other than the default 1.
  • Use distinct loop variables in nested loops so the control flow stays readable.

Don't

  • Omit the closing :NEXT; or use :EXITFOR; outside a :FOR ... :NEXT block.
  • Rely on changes to the original limit or step expressions inside the loop body.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Leaving out the required closing :NEXT; causes a parse error.
  • :EXITFOR; outside a :FOR loop causes a compile-time error with the message Found :EXITFOR outside :FOR.
  • The start, limit, and step values must be numeric at runtime.
  • If the start value is already past the limit for the chosen step direction, the loop body does not run.
  • A step of 0 prevents the loop variable from advancing, which can create a non-terminating loop when the initial condition is true.

docs.title: Iterating from 1 to 5
docs.text:
Runs the loop body for each integer from 1 to 5, printing a count message on each iteration.

:PROCEDURE CountToFive;
    :DECLARE nIndex, sMessage;

    :FOR nIndex := 1 :TO 5;
        sMessage := "Count is " + LimsString(nIndex);
        UsrMes(sMessage);
    :NEXT;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("CountToFive");

UsrMes displays:

Count is 1
Count is 2
Count is 3
Count is 4
Count is 5

docs.title: Using STEP to count by 2
docs.text:
:STEP changes the increment between iterations. The loop builds a comma-separated list of even numbers up to 10.

:PROCEDURE ListEvenNumbers;
    :DECLARE nIndex, nMax, sResult;

    nMax := 10;
    sResult := "Even numbers up to " + LimsString(nMax) + ": ";

    :FOR nIndex := 2 :TO nMax :STEP 2;
        :IF nIndex > 2;
            sResult := sResult + ", ";
        :ENDIF;

        sResult := sResult + LimsString(nIndex);
    :NEXT;

    UsrMes(sResult);
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("ListEvenNumbers");

UsrMes displays:

Even numbers up to 10: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10

docs.title: Counting backward and stopping early
docs.text:
A negative :STEP counts downward; :EXITFOR stops the loop as soon as the target is found. Searching from the end, nSampleId 1042 is at position 2.

:PROCEDURE FindPrioritySample;
    :DECLARE aSamples, nIndex, nSampleId, sStatus, sResult;
    :DECLARE bFound;

    aSamples := {
        {1001, "Normal"},
        {1042, "Critical"},
        {1087, "Normal"},
        {1105, "Normal"}
    };

    nSampleId := 1042;
    bFound := .F.;
    sResult := "Sample not found";

    :FOR nIndex := ALen(aSamples) :TO 1 :STEP -1;
        :IF aSamples[nIndex, 1] == nSampleId;
            sStatus := aSamples[nIndex, 2];

            :IF sStatus == "Critical";
                sResult := "Critical sample found at position " + LimsString(nIndex);
                bFound := .T.;
                :EXITFOR;
            :ENDIF;

            sResult := "Sample " + LimsString(nSampleId) + " has status: "
                + sStatus;
        :ENDIF;
    :NEXT;

    InfoMes(sResult);

    :RETURN bFound;
:ENDPROC;

/* Usage;
DoProc("FindPrioritySample");

InfoMes displays:

Critical sample found at position 2
